Tortious Conduct
ii) Requisite mental state; and iii) Causation. In contrast to the Third Restatement of Torts, the Second Restatement specifies the three elements as act, intent, and causation. You should be familiar with both sets of terminology.

rules.rule_statement: For battery and assault, the defendant’s tortious conduct must be an act. The act must be voluntary, meaning that the defendant must have directed the physical muscular movement. For false imprisonment and the intentional infliction of emotional distress, the focus is on the defendant’s conduct. Regarding the latter two torts, a defendant who fails to act when having a duty to do so may be liable, as may a defendant who affirmatively acts.

rules.source_text:
Tortious Conduct
For battery and assault, the defendant’s tortious conduct must be an act. The act must be voluntary, meaning that the defendant must have directed the physical muscular movement. For false imprisonment and the intentional infliction of emotional distress, the focus is on the defendant’s conduct. Regarding the latter two torts, a defendant who fails to act when having a duty to do so may be liable, as may a defendant who affirmatively acts.

rules.rule_statement: In most jurisdictions, neither a minor child nor a mentally impaired individual is excluded as such from liability for an intentional tort. Either may be liable if they act with the requisite mental state.
rules.short_rule_statement: In most jurisdictions, neither a minor child nor a mentally impaired individual is excluded as such from liability for an intentional tort. Either may be liable if they act...
rules.source_text:
Children And The Mentally Incompetent
In most jurisdictions, neither a minor child nor a mentally impaired individual is excluded as such from liability for an intentional tort. Either may be liable if they act with the requisite mental state.

rules.rule_statement: Transferred intent exists when a defendant intends to commit a battery, assault, or false imprisonment against one person but instead commits the intended tort against a different person. Transferred intent does not apply to transfer intent from an intentional tort based on personal injury (e.g., battery, assault) to an intentional tort based on harm to property (e.g., trespass to land). It also generally does not apply to intentional infliction of emotional distress but may under limited circumstances. See § I.D.1. Intent, infra. Example 1: An individual throws a punch intending to hit a friend. The punch misses the friend and instead lands on a bystander. The individual’s intent to commit an intentional tort (i.e., battery) against the friend satisfies the intent requirement for the same intentional tort against the bystander. Transferred intent also exists when a defendant intends to commit a battery (i.e., intends to cause a contact with the plaintiff’s person) and instead commits an assault (i.e., causes the plaintiff to anticipate an imminent and harmful or offensive contact). Example 2: An individual throws a ball at a friend with the intent to hit the friend. The friend anticipates that the ball will hit her, but it sails wide of its mark. The individual’s intent to commit one intentional tort (i.e., battery) satisfies the intent requirement for another intentional tort (i.e., assault). Similarly, transferred intent exists when a defendant intends to commit an assault and instead commits a battery. Example 3: An individual throws a ball in the direction of a friend, intending only to scare the friend. The ball hits the friend. The individual may be liable to the friend for battery. The individual’s intent to commit one intentional tort (i.e., assault) satisfies the intent requirement for another intentional tort (i.e., battery). However, even when a defendant’s actions are the factual cause of another’s harm, transferred intent does not apply if the harm that the plaintiff suffered is beyond the scope of liability. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 110.

rules.rule_statement: For the tort of intentional infliction of emotional distress, the requisite mental state can also be established if the defendant acts recklessly. Rule A defendant acts recklessly if: another in the person’s situation; and ii) The precaution that would eliminate or reduce the risk involves burdens that are so slight relative to the magnitude of the risk as to render the defendant’s failure to adopt the precaution a demonstration of the defendant’s indifference to the risk.

Intentional Inﬂiction Of Emotional Distress—Recklessness
For the tort of intentional infliction of emotional distress, the requisite mental state can also be established if the defendant acts recklessly. Rule A defendant acts recklessly if: another in the person’s situation; and ii) The precaution that would eliminate or reduce the risk involves burdens that are so slight relative to the magnitude of the risk as to render the defendant’s failure to adopt the precaution a demonstration of the defendant’s indifference to the risk.

rules.rule_statement: Causation exists when the defendant’s conduct is the legal cause of the resulting harm. To be the legal cause of the harm, the defendant’s conduct must be both the factual cause and the proximate cause of the harm.
rules.short_rule_statement: Causation exists when the defendant’s conduct is the legal cause of the resulting harm. To be the legal cause of the harm, the defendant’s conduct must be both the factual...
rules.source_text:
Causation
Causation exists when the defendant’s conduct is the legal cause of the resulting harm. To be the legal cause of the harm, the defendant’s conduct must be both the factual cause and the proximate cause of the harm.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant’s conduct is generally a factual cause of harm when the harm would not have occurred absent the conduct (i.e., but-for cause). When there are multiple acts, each of which by itself would have been a factual cause of the harm at the same time absent the other acts, each act is a factual cause of the harm.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A defendant’s conduct is generally a factual cause of harm when the harm would not have occurred absent the conduct (i.e., but-for cause). When there are multiple acts,...
rules.source_text:
Factual Cause
Rule A defendant’s conduct is generally a factual cause of harm when the harm would not have occurred absent the conduct (i.e., but-for cause). When there are multiple acts, each of which by itself would have been a factual cause of the harm at the same time absent the other acts, each act is a factual cause of the harm.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Proximate cause limits a defendant’s liability. A defendant who intentionally or recklessly causes harm is not subject to liability for harm the risk of which was not increased by the defendant’s intentional or reckless conduct. However, a defendant is not relieved from liability merely because the harm was unlikely to occur. In addition, a defendant who intentionally or recklessly causes harm is liable for a broader range of harms than the defendant would be if only acting negligently.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Proximate cause limits a defendant’s liability. A defendant who intentionally or recklessly causes harm is not subject to liability for harm the risk of which was not...
rules.source_text:
Proximate Cause
Rule Proximate cause limits a defendant’s liability. A defendant who intentionally or recklessly causes harm is not subject to liability for harm the risk of which was not increased by the defendant’s intentional or reckless conduct. However, a defendant is not relieved from liability merely because the harm was unlikely to occur. In addition, a defendant who intentionally or recklessly causes harm is liable for a broader range of harms than the defendant would be if only acting negligently.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant who knowingly and substantially instigates, encourages, or assists another person’s commission of an intentional tort involving personal injury is subject to liability for that tort, even if the actor’s conduct does not independently satisfy all elements of the underlying tort. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm §§ 26, 27. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm § 33. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 10.

rules.source_text:
Participation In An Intentional Tort
A defendant who knowingly and substantially instigates, encourages, or assists another person’s commission of an intentional tort involving personal injury is subject to liability for that tort, even if the actor’s conduct does not independently satisfy all elements of the underlying tort. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm §§ 26, 27. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm § 33. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 10.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant is subject to liability to the plaintiff for battery if: The defendant intends to cause a contact with the plaintiff’s person; The defendant’s affirmative conduct causes such a contact; and The contact causes bodily harm or is offensive to the plaintiff. For battery, a defendant’s intent to cause a contact with the plaintiff’s person may be satisfied by applying the transferred-intent doctrine to the defendant’s conduct. See § I.A.2.b. Transferred Intent, supra.

Battery
Rule A defendant is subject to liability to the plaintiff for battery if: The defendant intends to cause a contact with the plaintiff’s person; The defendant’s affirmative conduct causes such a contact; and The contact causes bodily harm or is offensive to the plaintiff. For battery, a defendant’s intent to cause a contact with the plaintiff’s person may be satisfied by applying the transferred-intent doctrine to the defendant’s conduct. See § I.A.2.b. Transferred Intent, supra.

rules.rule_statement: Most jurisdictions have not decided which party bears the burden of proof on the issue of consent, and the Restatement (Third) of Torts does not take a position. Most jurisdictions that have addressed the issue have placed the burden on the plaintiff to prove lack of actual consent. For battery, a plaintiff is generally precluded from recovery if she consents to either the harmful or offensive contact or the conduct by which the actor intends to cause such a contact.
rules.short_rule_statement: Most jurisdictions have not decided which party bears the burden of proof on the issue of consent, and the Restatement (Third) of Torts does not take a position. Most...
rules.source_text:
Lack Of Consent
Most jurisdictions have not decided which party bears the burden of proof on the issue of consent, and the Restatement (Third) of Torts does not take a position. Most jurisdictions that have addressed the issue have placed the burden on the plaintiff to prove lack of actual consent. For battery, a plaintiff is generally precluded from recovery if she consents to either the harmful or offensive contact or the conduct by which the actor intends to cause such a contact.

rules.rule_statement: Contact is harmful when it causes physical injury, illness, disease, impairment of bodily function, or death.
rules.short_rule_statement: Contact is harmful when it causes physical injury, illness, disease, impairment of bodily function, or death.
rules.source_text:
Harmful Contact
Contact is harmful when it causes physical injury, illness, disease, impairment of bodily function, or death.

rules.rule_statement: Contact is offensive when a person of ordinary sensibilities (i.e., a reasonable person) would find the contact offensive (objective test). Contact is also offensive when the defendant knows that the contact is highly offensive to the plaintiff’s sense of personal dignity and contacts the plaintiff with the primary purpose that the contact will be highly offensive, unless the court determines that imposing liability would violate public policy.
rules.short_rule_statement: Contact is offensive when a person of ordinary sensibilities (i.e., a reasonable person) would find the contact offensive (objective test). Contact is also offensive when the...
rules.source_text:
Offensive Contact
Contact is offensive when a person of ordinary sensibilities (i.e., a reasonable person) would find the contact offensive (objective test). Contact is also offensive when the defendant knows that the contact is highly offensive to the plaintiff’s sense of personal dignity and contacts the plaintiff with the primary purpose that the contact will be highly offensive, unless the court determines that imposing liability would violate public policy.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff need not be aware of the contact when it occurs to recover. Example: An operating room attendant inappropriately touches a patient while she is under the effect of anesthesia. The attendant is subject to liability for battery even though the patient was not aware of the touching.
rules.short_rule_statement: The plaintiff need not be aware of the contact when it occurs to recover. Example: An operating room attendant inappropriately touches a patient while she is under the effect...
rules.source_text:
Plaintiff’S Lack Of Awareness
The plaintiff need not be aware of the contact when it occurs to recover. Example: An operating room attendant inappropriately touches a patient while she is under the effect of anesthesia. The attendant is subject to liability for battery even though the patient was not aware of the touching.

rules.rule_statement: The harmful or offensive contact need not be with the defendant himself (e.g., the defendant throws a rock that strikes the plaintiff, the defendant pushes another individual into the plaintiff).
rules.short_rule_statement: The harmful or offensive contact need not be with the defendant himself (e.g., the defendant throws a rock that strikes the plaintiff, the defendant pushes another individual...
rules.source_text:
Indirect Contact
The harmful or offensive contact need not be with the defendant himself (e.g., the defendant throws a rock that strikes the plaintiff, the defendant pushes another individual into the plaintiff).

rules.canonical_name: Lack Of Contact—Purposeful Inﬂiction Of Bodily Harm
rules.rule_statement: When no contact (either direct or indirect) with the plaintiff’s person occurs, the defendant is not liable for battery. However, in rare cases, when the defendant’s conduct is particularly culpable, the Restatement (Third) of Torts imposes liability on the defendant for the purposeful infliction of bodily harm. Under the Restatement’s position, the defendant’s conduct could be either an affirmative act or a failure to act when the defendant has a duty to act. Example: The neighbor of an elderly individual knows that the individual has left the neighbor a small inheritance in his will. When the neighbor is present in the individual’s home, the individual has a heart attack. The individual attempts to reach for a phone to call 911. The neighbor, motived by the inheritance, seizes the phone before the individual can reach it and watches the individual die. The neighbor is not liable for battery because the neighbor has not made contact with the individual, but he is liable for the purposeful infliction of bodily harm.

rules.rule_statement: Contact with anything connected to the plaintiff’s person qualifies as contact with the plaintiff’s person for the purposes of battery (e.g., a person’s clothing, a pet held on a leash, a bicycle ridden by the plaintiff). For the contact to constitute a battery, contact with an object under the plaintiff’s physical control must be done with the necessary intent. Hitting a pet being walked by the plaintiff because the pet, on its own initiative, nipped the defendant would not constitute a battery.

Contact With Plaintiff’S Person
Contact with anything connected to the plaintiff’s person qualifies as contact with the plaintiff’s person for the purposes of battery (e.g., a person’s clothing, a pet held on a leash, a bicycle ridden by the plaintiff). For the contact to constitute a battery, contact with an object under the plaintiff’s physical control must be done with the necessary intent. Hitting a pet being walked by the plaintiff because the pet, on its own initiative, nipped the defendant would not constitute a battery.

rules.rule_statement: The defendant’s act, which must be both voluntary and affirmative, must result in harmful or offensive contact. A defendant who triggers a chain of events that causes contact with the plaintiff, whether the contact is direct or indirect, is subject to liability (e.g., the defendant sets a tripwire with the intent to cause the plaintiff to fall, and it causes the plaintiff to fall).

Causation
The defendant’s act, which must be both voluntary and affirmative, must result in harmful or offensive contact. A defendant who triggers a chain of events that causes contact with the plaintiff, whether the contact is direct or indirect, is subject to liability (e.g., the defendant sets a tripwire with the intent to cause the plaintiff to fall, and it causes the plaintiff to fall).

rules.rule_statement: Subject to the transferred-intent doctrine, the defendant must intend to cause a contact with the plaintiff. Defendant need only intend to cause contact. Defendant must intend to cause contact and that the contact be harmful or offensive.
rules.short_rule_statement: Subject to the transferred-intent doctrine, the defendant must intend to cause a contact with the plaintiff. Defendant need only intend to cause contact. Defendant must intend...

Intent
Subject to the transferred-intent doctrine, the defendant must intend to cause a contact with the plaintiff. Defendant need only intend to cause contact. Defendant must intend to cause contact and that the contact be harmful or offensive.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff may recover damages for physical injury (e.g., bodily harm) and for emotional distress (e.g., pain, suffering).
rules.short_rule_statement: A plaintiff may recover damages for physical injury (e.g., bodily harm) and for emotional distress (e.g., pain, suffering).
rules.source_text:
Damages
A plaintiff may recover damages for physical injury (e.g., bodily harm) and for emotional distress (e.g., pain, suffering).

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff may recover nominal damages to vindicate his right to physical autonomy, even though he did not suffer actual harm.
rules.short_rule_statement: A plaintiff may recover nominal damages to vindicate his right to physical autonomy, even though he did not suffer actual harm.

No Proof Of Actual Harm

rules.rule_statement: Many jurisdictions allow recovery of punitive damages if the defendant acted outrageously or with malice (i.e., a wrongful motive or a conscious or deliberate disregard of a high probability of harm). See § IV.F.7. Punitive Damages, infra.

Punitive Damages
Many jurisdictions allow recovery of punitive damages if the defendant acted outrageously or with malice (i.e., a wrongful motive or a conscious or deliberate disregard of a high probability of harm). See § IV.F.7. Punitive Damages, infra.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Under the thin-skull rule (also known as the “eggshell-skull” rule), the defendant is not required to foresee the extent of damages to be subject to liability for all damages. Example: A man inappropriately pinches a woman who has hemophilia. She later bleeds to death as a result of the act. The man is subject to liability for her death. Harm § 4. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 3. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 101 cmt. e. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 101 cmt. e. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 4.

Defendant’S Liability For Unforeseen Consequences
Rule Under the thin-skull rule (also known as the “eggshell-skull” rule), the defendant is not required to foresee the extent of damages to be subject to liability for all damages. Example: A man inappropriately pinches a woman who has hemophilia. She later bleeds to death as a result of the act. The man is subject to liability for her death. Harm § 4. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 3. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 101 cmt. e. rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant is subject to liability to the plaintiff for assault if: The defendant intends to cause the plaintiff to anticipate an imminent harmful or offensive contact with the plaintiff’s person; and The defendant’s affirmative conduct causes the plaintiff to anticipate such contact with the plaintiff’s person. Like battery, a defendant’s intent to cause a contact with the plaintiff’s person may be satisfied by applying the transferred-intent doctrine to defendant’s conduct. The Second Restatement of Torts focuses on whether the defendant intended to cause the plaintiff to apprehend an imminent harmful or offensive contact. The Third Restatement departs from “apprehend” and uses the term “anticipate.” You should be familiar with both terms.

Assault
Rule A defendant is subject to liability to the plaintiff for assault if: The defendant intends to cause the plaintiff to anticipate an imminent harmful or offensive contact with the plaintiff’s person; and The defendant’s affirmative conduct causes the plaintiff to anticipate such contact with the plaintiff’s person. Like battery, a defendant’s intent to cause a contact with the plaintiff’s person may be satisfied by applying the transferred-intent doctrine to defendant’s conduct. The Second Restatement of Torts focuses on whether the defendant intended to cause the plaintiff to apprehend an imminent harmful or offensive contact. The Third Restatement departs from “apprehend” and uses the term “anticipate.” You should be familiar with both terms.

rules.rule_statement: Unlike battery, contact with the plaintiff’s person is not required for assault. Example: A man throws a punch at a stranger and misses. The man may be subject to liability for assault even though he failed to land the punch.
rules.short_rule_statement: Unlike battery, contact with the plaintiff’s person is not required for assault. Example: A man throws a punch at a stranger and misses. The man may be subject to liability for...
rules.source_text:
No Actual Contact
Unlike battery, contact with the plaintiff’s person is not required for assault. Example: A man throws a punch at a stranger and misses. The man may be subject to liability for assault even though he failed to land the punch.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff must be aware of or have knowledge of the defendant’s act. A battery, by contrast, may be committed even though the plaintiff is unaware of the contact. Example: An individual kisses a sleeping stranger. The individual is not liable for assault even though the individual may be liable for battery. The anticipated contact must be harmful or offensive. Remember: The terms “anticipate” and “anticipation” in the Third Restatement of Torts are referred to as “apprehend” and “apprehension” in the Second Restatement. Under the majority rule, the standard for anticipation is objective. The plaintiff’s anticipation must be reasonable, meaning a reasonable person (i.e., a person of ordinary ability or courage) must have anticipated a harmful or offensive contact. Under the minority rule, which is supported by the Restatements, the standard for anticipation is subjective. The plaintiff must have anticipated a harmful or offensive contact. The minority rule recognizes that a plaintiff’s subjective anticipation of an imminent offensive contact cannot override the legal definition of an offensive contact. Although the traditional definition of assault uses the term “apprehension” rather than “anticipation,” the plaintiff need not fear the harmful or offensive contact. Example: A 6-year-old child attempts to kick an adult wearing body armor. The child may be subject to liability for assault even though the adult did not fear being harmed by the kick. The plaintiff’s ability to prevent the threatened contact does not preclude the plaintiff from anticipating that the defendant’s conduct would be harmful or offensive if the plaintiff failed to act. While a plaintiff may take preventive action, the plaintiff is not required to do so. Even though the plaintiff fails to take such action, the defendant is subject to liability for assault. Example 1: An elderly individual points a loaded gun at a teenager who is within arm’s length. The teenager can seize the gun before the individual can fire it. The individual may be liable for assault even though the teenager can prevent the individual from firing the gun. Similarly, a plaintiff’s knowledge that a third party may or will prevent the threatened harm does not preclude the defendant’s conduct from being an assault. Id. Example 2: An elementary school student cocks his arm to throw a rock at another student. The intended victim sees a teacher standing behind the student ready and able to prevent the student from throwing the rock. The student may be liable for assault even though the intended victim is certain that the teacher will intervene and prevent the student from throwing the rock. The defendant’s apparent ability to cause harm can be sufficient to cause the plaintiff to anticipate harm. Example: A woman threatens to shoot a man with a toy gun that appears to be a real gun. The woman may be liable for assault even though the gun cannot harm the man. A threat of contact with another individual does not constitute an assault of the plaintiff. Example: Leaning over a baby in a crib, an individual tells the baby’s mother, who is across the room, “I’m going to take your baby.” The individual is not liable for assault of the mother because the individual has not threatened contact with the mother.

rules.source_text:
Anticipation Of Contact
A plaintiff must be aware of or have knowledge of the defendant’s act. A battery, by contrast, may be committed even though the plaintiff is unaware of the contact. Example: An individual kisses a sleeping stranger. The individual is not liable for assault even though the individual may be liable for battery. The anticipated contact must be harmful or offensive. Remember: The terms “anticipate” and “anticipation” in the Third Restatement of Torts are referred to as “apprehend” and “apprehension” in the Second Restatement. Under the majority rule, the standard for anticipation is objective. The plaintiff’s anticipation must be reasonable, meaning a reasonable person (i.e., a person of ordinary ability or courage) must have anticipated a harmful or offensive contact. Under the minority rule, which is supported by the Restatements, the standard for anticipation is subjective. The plaintiff must have anticipated a harmful or offensive contact. The minority rule reco

rules.rule_statement: The threatened bodily harm or offensive contact must be imminent (i.e., without significant delay). Threats of future harm are usually insufficient, as are threats made by a defendant who cannot inflict imminent harm because of his physical distance from the plaintiff. However, a threat of future contact can satisfy the imminence requirement. Whether such a threat satisfies the requirement for imminent contact depends on the circumstances of the particular case and is ordinarily a question for the finder of fact. Example 1: A man calls someone 20 miles away and tells him, “I’m going to beat you up.” The man is likely not liable for assault because there will be a significant delay between the threat and the contact. Example 2: A man visits his ex-wife’s house and screams at her for several minutes. He then threatens to shoot her and walks out of the house toward his car. The ex-wife knows that the man always used to keep a gun in his car. Whether the ex-wife anticipated an imminent harmful or offensive contact would depend on the circumstances of the case and would be a question for the factfinder.

rules.rule_statement: Subject to the transferred-intent doctrine, the defendant must intend to cause the plaintiff to anticipate an imminent contact or substantially or virtually know that the plaintiff will experience such anticipation.
rules.short_rule_statement: Subject to the transferred-intent doctrine, the defendant must intend to cause the plaintiff to anticipate an imminent contact or substantially or virtually know that the...
rules.source_text:
Intent
Subject to the transferred-intent doctrine, the defendant must intend to cause the plaintiff to anticipate an imminent contact or substantially or virtually know that the plaintiff will experience such anticipation.

rules.rule_statement: It is sometimes said that “mere words alone do not constitute an assault.” However, words coupled with other acts or circumstances may be sufficient if the plaintiff reasonably anticipates that a harmful or offensive contact is imminent. Example 1: An individual sneaks up behind another in a dark alley and utters in a menacing voice, “Your money or your life.” The individual may be liable for assault even though the individual has taken no other action involving the other person. The defendant’s words, however, can negate the intent. Example 2: An individual raises his fists as if to hit a friend while saying, “If you weren’t such a good friend, I would punch you.” The individual’s words indicate a lack of intent to cause the friend to anticipate an imminent harmful or offensive contact.

Mere Words
It is sometimes said that “mere words alone do not constitute an assault.” However, words coupled with other acts or circumstances may be sufficient if the plaintiff reasonably anticipates that a harmful or offensive contact is imminent. Example 1: An individual sneaks up behind another in a dark alley and utters in a menacing voice, “Your money or your life.” The individual may be liable for assault even though the individual has taken no other action involving the other person. The defendant’s words, however, can negate the intent. Example 2: An individual raises his fists as if to hit a friend while saying, “If you weren’t such a good friend, I would punch you.” The individual’s words indicate a lack of intent to cause the friend to anticipate an imminent harmful or offensive contact.

rules.rule_statement: As with battery, a plaintiff may recover damages for physical injury (e.g., a heart attack) and for emotional distress (e.g., suffering) when the defendant is liable for assault. No proof of actual damages is required. The victim can recover nominal damages and, in appropriate cases, punitive damages. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 105 cmt. c. Id. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 105 cmt. d. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 105 cmt. c. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 105 cmt. f. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 105 cmt. g. Id.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant is subject to liability for the intentional infliction of emotional distress if the defendant by extreme and outrageous conduct intentionally or recklessly causes severe emotional distress to the plaintiff.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A defendant is subject to liability for the intentional infliction of emotional distress if the defendant by extreme and outrageous conduct intentionally or recklessly...
rules.source_text:
Intentional Infliction Of Emotional Distress
Rule A defendant is subject to liability for the intentional infliction of emotional distress if the defendant by extreme and outrageous conduct intentionally or recklessly causes severe emotional distress to the plaintiff.

rules.rule_statement: The defendant must intend to cause severe emotional distress or must act with recklessness as to the risk of causing such distress. The traditional doctrine of transferred intent does not apply to intentional infliction of emotional distress when the defendant intended to commit a different intentional tort (such as a battery) against a different victim. Instead, this situation is governed by the bystander rule for third-party victims. However, transferred intent may apply to intentional infliction of emotional distress if instead of harming the intended person, the defendant’s extreme and outrageous conduct harms another. See § 4. Emotional Harm Caused by Harm to Third Party, below. Example: With the intent to cause his ex-wife severe emotional distress, a man calls her cell phone to falsely tell her that their only child was murdered. However, the individual who answers the phone is not the man’s ex-wife but their child’s friend. The man’s intent to cause his ex-wife severe emotional distress satisfies the intent requirement for his liability to the friend for the intentional infliction of emotional distress.

Intent
The defendant must intend to cause severe emotional distress or must act with recklessness as to the risk of causing such distress. The traditional doctrine of transferred intent does not apply to intentional infliction of emotional distress when the defendant intended to commit a different intentional tort (such as a battery) against a different victim. Instead, this situation is governed by the bystander rule for third-party victims. However, transferred intent may apply to intentional infliction of emotional distress if instead of harming the intended person, the defendant’s extreme and outrageous conduct harms another. See § 4. Emotional Harm Caused by Harm to Third Party, below. Example: With the intent to cause his ex-wife severe emotional distress, a man calls her cell phone to falsely tell her that their only child was murdered. However, the individual who answers the phone is not the man’s ex-wife but their child’s friend. The man’s intent to cause his ex-wife severe em

rules.rule_statement: Conduct is extreme and outrageous if it exceeds the possible limits of human decency so as to be entirely intolerable in a civilized society. The character of the conduct must be outrageous, and the conduct must be sufficiently unusual to be extreme. Example: As a practical joke, the defendant tells an individual that his spouse was badly injured in an accident and is in the hospital. The acquaintance’s conduct satisfies the extreme and outrageous standard. While liability does not typically extend to mere insults, threats, or indignities, a defendant’s abusive language and conduct may be sufficiently extreme and outrageous if either: i) The defendant is in a position of authority or influence over the plaintiff (e.g., a police officer, employer, or school official, or traditionally an innkeeper or an employee of a common carrier); or ii) The plaintiff is a member of a group with a known heightened sensitivity (e.g., young children, pregnant women, or elderly persons).

Extreme And Outrageous Conduct
Conduct is extreme and outrageous if it exceeds the possible limits of human decency so as to be entirely intolerable in a civilized society. The character of the conduct must be outrageous, and the conduct must be sufficiently unusual to be extreme. Example: As a practical joke, the defendant tells an individual that his spouse was badly injured in an accident and is in the hospital. The acquaintance’s conduct satisfies the extreme and outrageous standard. rules.rule_statement: Public figures and public officials may not recover for the tort of intentional infliction of emotional distress by publication without showing that the publication contains a false statement of fact that was made with actual malice (i.e., with knowledge that the statement was false or with reckless disregard as to its truthfulness). In addition, the U.S. Supreme Court has suggested that private plaintiffs cannot recover for intentional infliction of emotional distress if the conduct at issue was speech on a matter of public concern because the First Amendment may protect that conduct.
rules.short_rule_statement: Public figures and public officials may not recover for the tort of intentional infliction of emotional distress by publication without showing that the publication contains a...
rules.source_text:
Public Figures
Public figures and public officials may not recover for the tort of intentional infliction of emotional distress by publication without showing that the publication contains a false statement of fact that was made with actual malice (i.e., with knowledge that the statement was false or with reckless disregard as to its truthfulness). In addition, the U.S. Supreme Court has suggested that private plaintiffs cannot recover for intentional infliction of emotional distress if the conduct at issue was speech on a matter of public concern because the First Amendment may protect that conduct.

rules.canonical_name: Emotional Harm Caused By Harm To Third Party
rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant who causes harm to an individual may be liable when his intentional or reckless conduct also causes severe emotional distress to the individual’s close family member who contemporaneously perceives the defendant’s extreme and outrageous conduct. A defendant must be at least reckless as to the risk that the conduct is substantially certain to cause severe emotional harm to the third party who contemporaneously perceives this conduct. This bystander rule does not require the close family member to suffer bodily harm. Example 1: An individual draws a pistol and threatens to shoot a woman in her husband’s presence, and the husband suffers severe emotional distress. The individual is subject to liability to the husband for intentional infliction of emotional distress. If the defendant’s purpose in harming an individual is to cause severe emotional distress to a third party, the third party need not have contemporaneously perceived the conduct nor be a close family member of the harmed individual. Example 2: A man kills a woman when the two are alone to cause severe emotional distress to the woman’s unrelated friend. When the friend learns of the killing the following day, the friend suffers severe emotional distress. The man is subject to liability to the friend for intentional infliction of emotional distress even though the friend was not a close family member of the woman or present at her killing.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff must establish that the defendant’s actions were a factual cause of the plaintiff’s distress.
rules.short_rule_statement: The plaintiff must establish that the defendant’s actions were a factual cause of the plaintiff’s distress.
rules.source_text:
Causation
The plaintiff must establish that the defendant’s actions were a factual cause of the plaintiff’s distress.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff must prove severe emotional distress beyond what a reasonable person could endure. If the defendant’s conduct also causes bodily harm to the plaintiff, the defendant may be liable for that harm as well. In many cases, the extreme and outrageous character of the defendant’s conduct itself provides evidence that the plaintiff experienced severe mental distress. In other words, the more extreme the defendant’s conduct is, the less evidence is required to prove the severity of the plaintiff’s emotional distress. If the plaintiff is hypersensitive, however, and experiences severe emotional distress unreasonably, then there is no liability unless the defendant knew of the plaintiff’s heightened sensitivity. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm § 46 cmt. d. Hustler Magazine v. Falwell, 485 U.S. 46, 56 (1988). Snyder v. Phelps, 562 U.S. 443 (2011). Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm § 46 cmt. m.

Damages
The plaintiff must prove severe emotional distress beyond what a reasonable person could endure. If the defendant’s conduct also causes bodily harm to the plaintiff, the defendant may be liable for that harm as well. In many cases, the extreme and outrageous character of the defendant’s conduct itself provides evidence that the plaintiff experienced severe mental distress. In other words, the more extreme the defendant’s conduct is, the less evidence is required to prove the severity of the plaintiff’s emotional distress. If the plaintiff is hypersensitive, however, and experiences severe emotional distress unreasonably, then there is no liability unless the defendant knew of the plaintiff’s heightened sensitivity. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm § 46 cmt. d. Hustler Magazine v. Falwell, 485 U.S. 46, 56 (1988). Snyder v. Phelps, 562 U.S. 443 (2011). rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant is subject to liability for false imprisonment if: The defendant intends to confine the plaintiff within a limited area; The defendant’s conduct causes the plaintiff’s confinement or the defendant fails to release the plaintiff from a confinement despite owing a duty to do so; and The plaintiff is conscious of the confinement. In a minority of jurisdictions, and under the Restatement (Third), a plaintiff who was not conscious of the confinement may still recover if the plaintiff was harmed by the confinement. In all jurisdictions, the intent element for false imprisonment may also be satisfied through the application of the transferred-intent doctrine.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A defendant is subject to liability for false imprisonment if: The defendant intends to confine the plaintiff within a limited area; The defendant’s conduct causes the...
rules.source_text:
False Imprisonment
Rule A defendant is subject to liability for false imprisonment if: The defendant intends to confine the plaintiff within a limited area; The defendant’s conduct causes the plaintiff’s confinement or the defendant fails to release the plaintiff from a confinement despite owing a duty to do so; and The plaintiff is conscious of the confinement. In a minority of jurisdictions, and under the Restatement (Third), a plaintiff who was not conscious of the confinement may still recover if the plaintiff was harmed by the confinement. In all jurisdictions, the intent element for false imprisonment may also be satisfied through the application of the transferred-intent doctrine.

rules.rule_statement: As with battery and assault, most jurisdictions have not decided which party bears the burden of proof on the issue of consent, and the Restatement (Third) of Torts has not taken a position. A majority of the jurisdictions that have addressed the issue place the burden on the plaintiff to prove lack of actual consent.
rules.short_rule_statement: As with battery and assault, most jurisdictions have not decided which party bears the burden of proof on the issue of consent, and the Restatement (Third) of Torts has not...
rules.source_text:
Lack Of Consent
As with battery and assault, most jurisdictions have not decided which party bears the burden of proof on the issue of consent, and the Restatement (Third) of Torts has not taken a position. A majority of the jurisdictions that have addressed the issue place the burden on the plaintiff to prove lack of actual consent.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, the plaintiff must be confined within a limited area in which the plaintiff’s freedom of movement in all directions is constrained. A plaintiff who is prevented from entering a building (e.g., store, home) is not confined, nor is a plaintiff who is prevented from traveling a particular route to reach a destination unless that route is the only means of reaching the destination. The area may be large (e.g., as large as a state, in some cases) and need not be stationary (e.g., a plaintiff trapped in a moving car). A plaintiff may also be confined when compelled to move in a highly restricted way, such as accompanying the defendant to the back of a store or to a police station.
rules.short_rule_statement: Generally, the plaintiff must be confined within a limited area in which the plaintiff’s freedom of movement in all directions is constrained. A plaintiff who is prevented from...
rules.source_text:
Within Limited Area
Generally, the plaintiff must be confined within a limited area in which the plaintiff’s freedom of movement in all directions is constrained. A plaintiff who is prevented from entering a building (e.g., store, home) is not confined, nor is a plaintiff who is prevented from traveling a particular route to reach a destination unless that route is the only means of reaching the destination. The area may be large (e.g., as large as a state, in some cases) and need not be stationary (e.g., a plaintiff trapped in a moving car). A plaintiff may also be confined when compelled to move in a highly restricted way, such as accompanying the defendant to the back of a store or to a police station.

rules.rule_statement: Assuming the other requirements of the tort are satisfied, a defendant is subject to liability for using one of following methods of confinement:
rules.short_rule_statement: Assuming the other requirements of the tort are satisfied, a defendant is subject to liability for using one of following methods of confinement:
rules.source_text:
Methods Of Conﬁnement
Assuming the other requirements of the tort are satisfied, a defendant is subject to liability for using one of following methods of confinement:

rules.rule_statement: ii) Physical force or restraint; iii) Threat of physical force or restraint; or iv) Duress (other than by threat of physical force or restraint). In addition to the above methods, confinement may occur by the defendant asserting legal authority. A defendant can confine a plaintiff by creating a physical barrier that precludes the plaintiff from exiting a limited area. The boundaries that confine the plaintiff need not be created by the defendant and often are not. Example: An individual, aware that another person is sitting in a windowless room, intentionally locks the only door to that room. The individual has confined the other person.
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) Physical force or restraint; iii) Threat of physical force or restraint; or iv) Duress (other than by threat of physical force or restraint). In addition to the above...
rules.source_text:
Physical Barriers
ii) Physical force or restraint; iii) Threat of physical force or restraint; or iv) Duress (other than by threat of physical force or restraint). In addition to the above methods, confinement may occur by the defendant asserting legal authority. A defendant can confine a plaintiff by creating a physical barrier that precludes the plaintiff from exiting a limited area. The boundaries that confine the plaintiff need not be created by the defendant and often are not. Example: An individual, aware that another person is sitting in a windowless room, intentionally locks the only door to that room. The individual has confined the other person.

components.content: Physical force or restraint or threat of physical force or restraint
components.source_text: 2)      Physical force or restraint or threat of physical force or restraint

components.content: The defendant asserts the legal authority to take the plaintiff into custody or to otherwise confine the plaintiff;
components.label: i)
components.source_text: i) The defendant asserts the legal authority to take the plaintiff into custody or to otherwise confine the plaintiff;

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff who knows of a readily available, feasible, and safe way to exit the limited area is not confined by the physical barrier. However, a plaintiff is not required to use an exit that would expose her to any risk of physical harm. Example 1: An individual, aware that another person is sitting in a room on the ground floor of a building, intentionally locks the only door to that room. The room has a window through which the other person can safely exit the room without any risk of physical harm. The individual has not confined the other person. Example 2: An individual, aware that another person is sitting in a room on the second floor of a building, intentionally locks the only door to that room. The room has a window through which the other person can exit the room, but in doing so, the other person would risk a sprained ankle. The individual has confined the other person. Although a defendant’s lack of knowledge of such an exit may indicate that the defendant had the intent to confine the plaintiff, the defendant’s conduct does not result in the plaintiff’s confinement if the plaintiff has such knowledge. Example 3: An individual, aware that another person is sitting in a windowless room, intentionally locks what the individual believes is the only door to that room. The other person is aware that a panel of the wall can be opened to access a safe exit from the room. The individual has not confined the other person. A defendant who employs physical force or restraint against a plaintiff to prevent the plaintiff from leaving a limited area has confined the plaintiff. If physical force or restraint employed wrongfully places the plaintiff at risk of being subjected to additional force, restraint, indignity, or physical injury, the plaintiff who submits to the force or restraint has been confined even though the plaintiff is physically capable of escaping. Example: A 10-year-old, 70-lb fan grabs a professional football player’s shirt as the player walks across a stadium parking lot after a game. The fan intends to retain his hold until the player signs the fan’s football. Although the player could easily break free of the fan’s grasp, the player does not. The fan has confined the football player. A defendant who threatens to employ immediate physical force or restraint if the plaintiff attempts to leave the limited area has confined the plaintiff. The threat may be expressed or implied and may be made by words, conduct, or both. A plaintiff who agrees to remain in a limited area because of the defendant’s duress has been confined. The duress may arise from the defendant’s threat to harm a member of the plaintiff’s family or retain the plaintiff’s property, but not all threats or forms of pressure (e.g., moral pressure) constitute duress. A defendant confines a plaintiff if: and ii) The plaintiff submits to such confinement because she believes that she either:

rules.rule_statement: Although the defendant may have committed false imprisonment by such confinement, the defendant’s conduct may be privileged. See § II.H. Privilege of Arrest and Other Crime-Related Conduct, infra. Improper confinement occurs when the defendant refuses to perform a duty to release the plaintiff from an existing confinement or to provide the plaintiff with a means of escape. Example: An individual accidentally locks herself in a restaurant’s restroom. The restaurant may be liable if employees intentionally fail to assist her in unlocking the door.

Has A Duty To Comply With The Assertion Of Authority;
Although the defendant may have committed false imprisonment by such confinement, the defendant’s conduct may be privileged. See § II.H. Privilege of Arrest and Other Crime-Related Conduct, infra. Improper confinement occurs when the defendant refuses to perform a duty to release the plaintiff from an existing confinement or to provide the plaintiff with a means of escape. Example: An individual accidentally locks herself in a restaurant’s restroom. The restaurant may be liable if employees intentionally fail to assist her in unlocking the door.

rules.rule_statement: In most jurisdictions, the plaintiff must be aware of the confinement when it occurs. In a minority of jurisdictions and under the Restatement (Third), the plaintiff must either be aware of the confinement when it occurs or suffer actual harm from the confinement.

rules.source_text:
Knowledge Of Conﬁnement
In most jurisdictions, the plaintiff must be aware of the confinement when it occurs. In a minority of jurisdictions and under the Restatement (Third), the plaintiff must either be aware of the confinement when it occurs or suffer actual harm from the confinement.

rules.rule_statement: The defendant must act with the purpose of confining the plaintiff or knowing that the plaintiff’s confinement is substantially certain to result. The defendant may still possess the necessary intent despite acting under the belief that the confinement is in the plaintiff’s best interest or that the plaintiff will welcome the confinement. However, in such a case, the defendant may not be liable to the plaintiff because of a defense (e.g., consent). Contrast with negligence and accident: If the confinement is due to the defendant’s negligence rather than his intentional acts, then the defendant may be liable for negligence but not false imprisonment. If the imprisonment occurs by pure accident and involves neither the defendant’s intent nor his negligence, then there is no recovery.

Intent
The defendant must act with the purpose of confining the plaintiff or knowing that the plaintiff’s confinement is substantially certain to result. The defendant may still possess the necessary intent despite acting under the belief that the confinement is in the plaintiff’s best interest or that the plaintiff will welcome the confinement. However, in such a case, the defendant may not be liable to the plaintiff because of a defense (e.g., consent). Contrast with negligence and accident: If the confinement is due to the defendant’s negligence rather than his intentional acts, then the defendant may be liable for negligence but not false imprisonment. If the imprisonment occurs by pure accident and involves neither the defendant’s intent nor his negligence, then there is no recovery.

rules.rule_statement: Proving actual damages is unnecessary unless, in a minority of jurisdictions, the plaintiff is unaware of the confinement, in which case the plaintiff must suffer harm. The victim can recover nominal damages and, in appropriate cases, punitive damages. The thin-skull rule applies; a defendant may be liable for harm to the plaintiff that the defendant did not anticipate. Id. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 8 cmt. e. The victim can recover nominal damages and, in appropriate cases, punitive damages. The thin-skull rule applies; a defendant may be liable for harm to the plaintiff that the defendant did not anticipate. Id. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Intentional Torts to Persons § 8 cmt. e. rules.rule_statement: ii) Apparent consent; iii) Presumed consent; or iv) The emergency doctrine. For the intentional torts of battery, assault, and false imprisonment, most jurisdictions have not decided which party bears the burden of proof on the issue of consent, and the Restatement (Third) has not taken a position. Most jurisdictions that have addressed the issue have placed the burden of proof on the plaintiff to prove lack of actual consent. Under the Third Restatement of Torts, actual consent is a defense to an intentional tort. The Second Restatement of Torts refers to “actual consent” as “express consent.” In addition, note that the Third Restatement uses “presumed consent” instead of “implied consent.” You should be familiar with both changes to the consent terminology.

Most jurisdictions that have addressed the issue have placed the burden of proof on the plaintiff to prove lack of actual consent. Under the Third Restatement of Torts, actual consent is a defense to an intentional tort. The Second Restatement of Torts refers to “actual consent” as “express consent.” In addition, note that the Third Restatement uses “presumed consent” instead of “implied consent.” You should be familiar with both changes to the consent terminology.

Actual Consent
Rule A plaintiff actually consents to the defendant’s otherwise tortious intentional conduct if the plaintiff is willing for that conduct to occur. Such willingness may be express or inferred from the facts.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff’s actual consent extends to conduct by the defendant that does not substantially differ in nature from the conduct that the plaintiff is willing to permit. When a plaintiff places a condition on consent that limits the consent in time, place, or otherwise, the consent is legally effective only within the limits of that condition. When a plaintiff clearly communicates a revocation of his actual consent to the defendant, the consent is generally no longer legally effective. An exception exists when it would be unreasonably burdensome for the defendant to immediately comply with the revocation of consent. Jurisdictions are split as to whether a plaintiff may consent to a crime, with the modern trend and the Third Restatement of Torts recognizing that a plaintiff may do so. However, even in jurisdictions that recognize that a plaintiff may consent to a crime, the consent is not legally effective if the plaintiff is a member of a class that the crime is intended to protect, regardless of consent (e.g., the consent of a victim of statutory rape is not legally effective). A plaintiff’s actual consent does not extend to past conduct.

Scope Conditions

When a plaintiff clearly communicates a revocation of his actual consent to the defendant, the consent is generally no longer legally effective. An exception exists when it would be unreasonably burdensome for the defendant to immediately comply with the revocation of consent. Jurisdictions are split as to whether a plaintiff may consent to a crime, with the modern trend and the Third Restatement of Torts recognizing that a plaintiff may do so. However, even in jurisdictions that recognize that a plaintiff may consent to a crime, the consent is not legally effective if the plaintiff is a member of a class that the crime is intended

rules.rule_statement: If the plaintiff gives actual consent because of a substantial mistake regarding the nature of the invasion of the plaintiff’s interests, the extent of the expected harm, or the purpose of the defendant’s conduct, the consent is nevertheless valid. However, this consent is not valid if the defendant caused the mistake by affirmative misrepresentation or fraud or if she knew of the mistake.

rules.source_text:
Mistake
If the plaintiff gives actual consent because of a substantial mistake regarding the nature of the invasion of the plaintiff’s interests, the extent of the expected harm, or the purpose of the defendant’s conduct, the consent is nevertheless valid. However, this consent is not valid if the defendant caused the mistake by affirmative misrepresentation or fraud or if she knew of the mistake.

rules.rule_statement: Actual consent given under duress (e.g., physical force or threats) is not valid. The threat, however, must be of present action, not of future action. In general, neither threats of economic duress nor moral pressure render a plaintiff’s consent invalid.

rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff’s lack of capacity due to youth, intoxication, or intellectual incompetence may negate the validity of consent. However, an individual who appreciates the nature, extent, and potential consequences of the conduct has the capacity to consent to it. Generally, an adult is rebuttably presumed to have the capacity to consent.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Under the doctrine of presumed consent, a defendant is not liable for otherwise tortious intentional conduct if: i) Under prevailing social norms, the defendant is justified in engaging in the conduct in the absence of the plaintiff’s actual or apparent consent; and ii) The defendant has no reason to believe that the plaintiff would not have actually consented to the conduct had the defendant requested the plaintiff’s consent. This category of consent is also known as “implied consent.”

This category of consent is also known as “implied consent.”

rules.canonical_name: Participant In Athletic And Recreational Activities
rules.rule_statement: Whether a participant in an athletic or recreational activity can rely on apparent or presumed consent is determined using a variety of factors. These factors include whether the conduct violates the activity’s safety rules, whether the conduct typically occurs during the activity, and whether the conduct involves significant risks of very serious injury or death. Some jurisdictions prohibit recovery unless the defendant was reckless regarding the risk of physical harm or acted with the purpose to injure. Other jurisdictions preclude liability for harm resulting from the inherent risks of the game or activity.

Participant In Athletic And Recreational Activities

Some jurisdictions prohibit recovery unless the defendant was reckless regarding the risk of physical harm or acted with the purpose to injure. Other jurisdictions preclude liability for harm resulting from the inherent risks of the game or activity.

rules.rule_statement: The general consent rules apply to sexual conduct. In addition, when a defendant causes a nonconsensual sexual contact with a plaintiff, the contact constitutes an offensive contact for purposes of battery. Also, when a plaintiff expresses through words or conduct an unwillingness to engage in a sexual act but the defendant nevertheless causes the person to submit to or perform the act, the plaintiff has not consented to the act, and the defendant is liable for the applicable intentional tort.

Consent To Sexual Conduct

In addition, when a defendant causes a nonconsensual sexual contact with a plaintiff, the contact constitutes an offensive contact for purposes of battery. Also, when a plaintiff expresses through words or conduct an unwillingness to engage in a sexual act but the defendant nevertheless causes the person to submit to or perform the act, the plaintiff has not consented to the act, and the defendant is liable for the applicable intentional tort.

rules.canonical_name: Medical Treatment Without Legally Effective Consent As Battery
rules.rule_statement: Most jurisdictions treat a physician’s failure to adequately disclose the risks and benefits of a medical procedure as giving rise to a negligence claim (often labeled a “lack of informed consent”). Most jurisdictions treat a physician’s failure to secure a patient’s consent to the nature, type, or extent of physical contact that the physician intentionally causes as a battery (e.g., a patient consents to operation on his left foot, but the physician operates on the right foot). rules.rule_statement: The following privileges are defenses to the intentional torts of battery, assault, and false imprisonment. Although most often the privileges arise as a defense to battery that is attributable to the defendant’s use of force, the defendant’s tortious conduct may result from the defendant’s threat of force (i.e., assault) or confinement of another (i.e., false imprisonment). The use of the term “force” in reference to the defendant’s conduct and the applicability of a privilege encompasses the threat of force as well as confinement.
rules.short_rule_statement: The following privileges are defenses to the intentional torts of battery, assault, and false imprisonment. Although most often the privileges arise as a defense to battery...
rules.source_text:
Privileges--In General
The following privileges are defenses to the intentional torts of battery, assault, and false imprisonment. Although most often the privileges arise as a defense to battery that is attributable to the defendant’s use of force, the defendant’s tortious conduct may result from the defendant’s threat of force (i.e., assault) or confinement of another (i.e., false imprisonment). The use of the term “force” in reference to the defendant’s conduct and the applicability of a privilege encompasses the threat of force as well as confinement.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant has a privilege to use force against the plaintiff to defend himself against the plaintiff’s unprivileged use of force if the defendant reasonably believes that the force is both necessary and proportionate to the force that the plaintiff is intentionally inflicting or about to inflict.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A defendant has a privilege to use force against the plaintiff to defend himself against the plaintiff’s unprivileged use of force if the defendant reasonably believes...
rules.source_text:
Self-Defense
Rule A defendant has a privilege to use force against the plaintiff to defend himself against the plaintiff’s unprivileged use of force if the defendant reasonably believes that the force is both necessary and proportionate to the force that the plaintiff is intentionally inflicting or about to inflict.

rules.rule_statement: The defendant’s use of force must be for a defensive purpose. When a defendant’s sole purpose is to retaliate for past conduct or to deter future conduct, the defendant’s use of force is not privileged. However, a defensive purpose need not be the defendant’s sole motive for using force. The defendant’s use of force may be privileged even though the defendant is also using force because of a personal dislike of or hostility toward the plaintiff.

Defensive Purpose

When a defendant’s sole purpose is to retaliate for past conduct or to deter future conduct, the defendant’s use of force is not privileged. However, a defensive purpose need not be the defendant’s sole motive for using force. The defendant’s use of force may be privileged even though the defendant is also using force because of a personal dislike of or hostility toward the plaintiff.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant may use nondeadly force to defend himself against another only if the defendant reasonably believes that: ii) The defendant’s force is proportionate to the other’s force or threat of force; and iii) The defendant can prevent the other’s force or threat of force only by the defendant’s immediate use of the force.

Use Of Nondeadly Force
Rule A defendant may use nondeadly force to defend himself against another only if the defendant reasonably believes that: ii) The defendant’s force is proportionate to the other’s force or threat of force; and iii) The defendant can prevent the other’s force or threat of force only by the defendant’s immediate use of the force.

rules.rule_statement: Nondeadly force is force neither intended nor likely to cause death or serious bodily harm. Serious bodily harm is serious, permanent disfigurement or permanent or protracted loss or impairment of the function of any important bodily member or organ.
rules.short_rule_statement: Nondeadly force is force neither intended nor likely to cause death or serious bodily harm. Serious bodily harm is serious, permanent disfigurement or permanent or protracted...
rules.source_text:
Nondeadly Force
Nondeadly force is force neither intended nor likely to cause death or serious bodily harm. Serious bodily harm is serious, permanent disfigurement or permanent or protracted loss or impairment of the function of any important bodily member or organ.

rules.rule_statement: When a defendant uses nondeadly force, there is no duty to retreat even if the defendant can do so safely. Similarly, the defendant is not required to give up a right (e.g., to use a public space) or privilege (e.g., to defend himself or a third person).
rules.short_rule_statement: When a defendant uses nondeadly force, there is no duty to retreat even if the defendant can do so safely. Similarly, the defendant is not required to give up a right (e.g., to...
rules.source_text:
No Duty To Retreat
When a defendant uses nondeadly force, there is no duty to retreat even if the defendant can do so safely. Similarly, the defendant is not required to give up a right (e.g., to use a public space) or privilege (e.g., to defend himself or a third person).

rules.rule_statement: When the defendant has consented to the plaintiff’s use of force, the defendant does not have the privilege of self-defense unless the plaintiff exceeds the scope of the defendant’s consent. Similarly, if the plaintiff is privileged to use force against the defendant (e.g., self-defense), then the defendant does not have a privilege to respond with force.
rules.short_rule_statement: When the defendant has consented to the plaintiff’s use of force, the defendant does not have the privilege of self-defense unless the plaintiff exceeds the scope of the...
rules.source_text:
Plaintiff’S Unprivileged Use Of Force
When the defendant has consented to the plaintiff’s use of force, the defendant does not have the privilege of self-defense unless the plaintiff exceeds the scope of the defendant’s consent. Similarly, if the plaintiff is privileged to use force against the defendant (e.g., self-defense), then the defendant does not have a privilege to respond with force.

rules.rule_statement: For the defendant’s use of force to be privileged, the plaintiff must either be using force against the defendant or threatening to immediately use force against the defendant. In addition, the defendant’s use of force must be an immediate response to the plaintiff’s use or threat of force. However, the defendant need not wait until the last second to respond to a threatened use of force in the near future if there will not be a later opportunity to prevent the attack. Spoken words or verbal gestures (e.g., provocative language, insults) do not by themselves justify the use of force. Neither does a verbal threat of immediate physical harm unless there is additional conduct either at that time (e.g., taking a step toward the defendant) or in the past (e.g., inflicting physical harm after making such a threat) that demonstrates that the plaintiff intends is to immediately act on the threat. Alternatively, as with battery, words may negate a threat (e.g., “If we were in the Middle Ages, I’d stab you with this sword.”).
rules.short_rule_statement: For the defendant’s use of force to be privileged, the plaintiff must either be using force against the defendant or threatening to immediately use force against the defendant....
rules.source_text:
Immediacy
For the defendant’s use of force to be privileged, the plaintiff must either be using force against the defendant or threatening to immediately use force against the defendant. In addition, the defendant’s use of force must be an immediate response to the plaintiff’s use or threat of force. However, the defendant need not wait until the last second to respond to a threatened use of force in the near future if there will not be a later opportunity to prevent the attack. Spoken words or verbal gestures (e.g., provocative language, insults) do not by themselves justify the use of force. Neither does a verbal threat of immediate physical harm unless there is additional conduct either at that time (e.g., taking a step toward the defendant) or in the past (e.g., inflicting physical harm after making such a threat) that demonstrates that the plaintiff intends is to immediately act on the threat. Alternatively, as with battery, words may negate a threat (e.g., “If we were in the Midd

rules.rule_statement: Regarding the use of nondeadly force, the force used by the defendant should not be significantly greater than the force used by the plaintiff. The defendant is not privileged to use force that is greater in degree or kind than the force the defendant knows or should know will prevent the threatened harm. In addition, if the defendant knows that the plaintiff’s threatened use of force is based on a mistake, the defendant is not privileged to use force if the defendant has time to correct the mistake and prevent the attack.
rules.short_rule_statement: Regarding the use of nondeadly force, the force used by the defendant should not be significantly greater than the force used by the plaintiff. The defendant is not privileged...
rules.source_text:
Proportional Force
Regarding the use of nondeadly force, the force used by the defendant should not be significantly greater than the force used by the plaintiff. The defendant is not privileged to use force that is greater in degree or kind than the force the defendant knows or should know will prevent the threatened harm. In addition, if the defendant knows that the plaintiff’s threatened use of force is based on a mistake, the defendant is not privileged to use force if the defendant has time to correct the mistake and prevent the attack.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant who is mistaken as to the need to immediately use force is nevertheless privileged to use force if the defendant reasonably believes, considering the exigent circumstances, that relevant facts exist that would justify the defendant’s use of force.
rules.short_rule_statement: A defendant who is mistaken as to the need to immediately use force is nevertheless privileged to use force if the defendant reasonably believes, considering the exigent...
rules.source_text:
Reasonable Belief
A defendant who is mistaken as to the need to immediately use force is nevertheless privileged to use force if the defendant reasonably believes, considering the exigent circumstances, that relevant facts exist that would justify the defendant’s use of force.
