rules.rule_statement: Strict liability for an abnormally dangerous activity exists if the harm results from the risk that made the activity abnormally dangerous in the first place. Example: A defendant drops a heavy package of explosives on the plaintiff’s foot, severely injuring it. The injury did not result from the risk of an explosion, which is the risk that makes the use of explosives an abnormally dangerous activity. As with negligence, the defendant’s liability can be cut off by unforeseeable intervening causes. See § IV.E.3.b.2.b) Unforeseeable Intervening Causes, supra.

rules.short_rule_statement: The Second Restatement of Torts defined wild animals as animals in a category that is “not by custom devoted to the service of mankind [humankind] at the time and in the place...
rules.source_text:
Wild Animals
The Second Restatement of Torts defined wild animals as animals in a category that is “not by custom devoted to the service of mankind [humankind] at the time and in the place in which it is kept.” The Third Restatement narrows this definition by excluding animals that pose no obvious risk of causing substantial personal injury. Under the Third Restatement, a wild animal is an animal that belongs to a category of animals (e.g., species) that:

rules.canonical_name: Have Not Been Generally Domesticated In The United States;
rules.rule_statement: ii) Are likely, unless restrained, to cause personal injury. Example 1: An elephant in the United States that has been tamed and exhibited as part of a circus strikes a circus acrobat with its trunk. The elephant is categorized as a wild animal even though, in other countries, it has been domesticated. Because an unrestrained elephant is likely to cause personal injury because of its strength and temperament, an elephant would be a wild animal under both the Second and Third Restatements. Example 2: A pet snake escapes its enclosure and bites its owner’s roommate. Snakes are not generally domesticated in the United States and would be considered wild animals under the Second Restatement. However, under the Third Restatement, whether the snake is considered wild depends on the particular species of snake; a garter snake would not be considered wild because its species is not likely to cause personal injury, but a rattlesnake, even if defanged, would be considered wild because it belongs to a species of animals that are likely to cause personal injury. Under both definitions, a wild animal remains a wild animal despite being tamed for several years and even though its departure from that tameness is sudden and unexpected. Example 3: A veterinarian raised a young chimpanzee as a pet. She kept the chimpanzee in her home and diligently trained and socialized it for many years. The chimpanzee has always adapted easily to meeting new people and has never injured anyone. One day, without warning, the chimpanzee bites a visitor to the veterinarian’s house. Because the chimpanzee is a wild animal, strict liability will apply.
rules.short_rule_statement: ii) Are likely, unless restrained, to cause personal injury. Example 1: An elephant in the United States that has been tamed and exhibited as part of a circus strikes a circus...
rules.source_text:
Have Not Been Generally Domesticated In The United States;
ii) Are likely, unless restrained, to cause personal injury. Example 1: An elephant in the United States that has been tamed and exhibited as part of a circus strikes a circus acrobat with its trunk. The elephant is categorized as a wild animal even though, in other countries, it has been domesticated. Because an unrestrained elephant is likely to cause personal injury because of its strength and temperament, an elephant would be a wild animal under both the Second and Third Restatements. Example 2: A pet snake escapes its enclosure and bites its owner’s roommate. Snakes are not generally domesticated in the United States and would be considered wild animals under the Second Restatement. However, under the Third Restatement, whether the snake is considered wild depends on the particular species of snake; a garter snake would not be considered wild because its species is not likely to cause personal injury, but a rattlesnake, ev

rules.rule_statement: Rule The owner or possessor of an animal is strictly liable for injuries caused by that animal if: the animal’s category or species; and ii) The harm results from those dangerous propensities. Otherwise, under common law, the owner of a domestic animal is generally liable only for negligence. Example: A plaintiff suffered injuries when he was thrown from his neighbor’s young horse. Even if the neighbor knew that the young horse was high strung and skittish, these traits are unlikely to establish that the neighbor knew or had reason to know that the horse had abnormally dangerous propensities because of how common it is for horses to have these traits. However, if the neighbor knew that the horse had an unusual habit of throwing all its riders, strict liability may apply.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The owner or possessor of any animal, wild or domestic (other than household pets), is strictly liable for any reasonably foreseeable damage caused by the animal while the animal is trespassing on the land of another. The exception for household pets (the Third Restatement specifically mentions dogs and cats) does not apply if the owner knows or has reason to know that the dog or cat is intruding on another’s property in a way that tends to cause substantial harm. The general negligence standard applies if an animal strays onto a public road and contributes to an accident there.

rules.rule_statement: In most jurisdictions, the landlord is not liable for harms caused by animals owned by his tenants because the landlord lacks the required element of control over the animal. Some jurisdictions impose liability on the landlord based on negligence if the landlord is aware of the dangerous propensities of the dog or other animal and has control under the terms of the lease of the tenant’s possession of animals. Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm § 21.

rules.rule_statement: A product may be defective because of a defect in its design or manufacture or because of a failure to adequately warn the consumer of a hazard related to the foreseeable use of the product. When a plaintiff files a products-liability suit, there are at least three possible claims on which to base an action: Negligence Strict products liability Breach of warranty As discussed below, each type of claim in a products-liability case requires different elements. Significantly, the Third Restatement provides for only a single cause of action in the absence of additional facts. Some courts have begun to adopt the Third Restatement’s approach. Keep in mind that products-liability cases are not limited to negligence, strict products liability, and breach of warranty. If the defendant intended or knew with substantial certainty the consequences of the defect, then the cause of action could be based on an intentional tort. As with any intentional tort claim, punitive as well as compensatory damages are recoverable. The same defenses germane to each type of tort are applicable.

Products Liability

rules.rule_statement: The commercial manufacturer, distributor, retailer, or seller of a product owes a duty of reasonable care to any foreseeable plaintiff (i.e., a purchaser, user, or bystander).
rules.short_rule_statement: The commercial manufacturer, distributor, retailer, or seller of a product owes a duty of reasonable care to any foreseeable plaintiff (i.e., a purchaser, user, or bystander).
rules.source_text:
Duty
The commercial manufacturer, distributor, retailer, or seller of a product owes a duty of reasonable care to any foreseeable plaintiff (i.e., a purchaser, user, or bystander).

rules.rule_statement: Failure to exercise reasonable care in the inspection or sale of a product constitutes breach of that duty. The plaintiff must establish not only that the defect exists but also that the defendant’s negligent conduct (lack of reasonable care) led to the plaintiff’s harm. In other words, the plaintiff must establish that had the defendant exercised reasonable care in the inspection or sale of the product, the defect would have been discovered, and the plaintiff would not have been harmed. The plaintiff also has the option of invoking res ipsa loquitur if the defect could not have occurred without the manufacturer’s negligence. The individual defendant must have breached his duty to reasonably inspect or sell. Unlike strict products liability, the negligence of others in the supply chain cannot be imputed. Rather, the plaintiff has the burden of proving fault on the part of any particular defendant.
rules.short_rule_statement: Failure to exercise reasonable care in the inspection or sale of a product constitutes breach of that duty. The plaintiff must establish not only that the defect exists but...
rules.source_text:
Breach
Failure to exercise reasonable care in the inspection or sale of a product constitutes breach of that duty. The plaintiff must establish not only that the defect exists but also that the defendant’s negligent conduct (lack of reasonable care) led to the plaintiff’s harm. In other words, the plaintiff must establish that had the defendant exercised reasonable care in the inspection or sale of the product, the defect would have been discovered, and the plaintiff would not have been harmed. The plaintiff also has the option of invoking res ipsa loquitur if the defect could not have occurred without the manufacturer’s negligence. The individual defendant must have breached his duty to reasonably inspect or sell. Unlike strict products liability, the negligence of others in the supply chain cannot be imputed. Rather, the plaintiff has the burden of proving fault on the part of any particular defendant.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff must prove factual and proximate causation. See IV.E. Causation, supra. When a retailer sells a product with a known defect and without giving adequate warnings about the defect, the failure to warn may be a superseding cause, breaking the chain of causation between the manufacturer and the injury.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff is entitled to recover damages resulting from any personal injury or property damage. rules.rule_statement: Depending on the jurisdiction, courts apply either the consumer-expectation test or the risk-utility test to determine whether a design defect exists. Many jurisdictions use various hybrids of the two tests, and some states allow the plaintiff to prove a design defect under either test. Design Defect Tests Does the product include a condition not contemplated by Consumer-expectation test the ordinary consumer that is unreasonably dangerous to the consumer? Do the risks posed by the product outweigh its Risk-utility test benefits? Under the risk-utility test, in a majority of jurisdictions and under the Third Restatement, the plaintiff must prove that a reasonable alternative design was available to the defendant and the failure to use that design has rendered the product not reasonably safe. The alternative design must be economically feasible. Merely providing a warning does not necessarily prevent a product from being unreasonably dangerous.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A failure?to?warn defect exists for a product if there were foreseeable risks of harm not obvious to an ordinary user of the product and the risks could have been reduced or avoided by providing reasonable instructions or warnings. An action brought under a failure-to-warn theory is essentially the same as a design-defect claim, but the defect in question is the manufacturer’s failure to provide an adequate warning related to the risks of using the product. The failure to include the instructions or warnings renders the product not reasonably safe. Under the learned-intermediary rule, the manufacturer of a prescription drug or medical device typically satisfies its duty to warn the consumer by informing the prescribing physician, rather than the patient, of problems with the drug or device. There are several exceptions, including, most importantly: intervention or evaluation of a healthcare provider, such as when a vaccine is administered through a mass inoculation; and ii) As a result of a federal statute, in the case of birth control pills.

rules.rule_statement: To bring a strict-liability action, a plaintiff is not required to be in privity of contract with the defendant. Anyone foreseeably injured by a defective product or whose property is harmed by the product may bring a strict-liability action. Appropriate plaintiffs include not only purchasers but also other users of the product and even bystanders who suffer personal injury or property damage.

rules.rule_statement: If the seller is a commercial supplier of the product, the seller is subject to strict liability for a defective product, even if the revenue from sales of the product is not a significant portion of its business. The seller is generally strictly liable even if the seller was not responsible for the defect in any way and even when the product is not purchased directly from the seller. However, a retail seller or other distributor of a prescription drug or medical device is subject to liability for harm caused by the drug or device only if: ii) At or before the time of sale or other distribution of the drug or medical device, the retail seller or other distributor fails to exercise reasonable care and such failure causes harm to persons.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The commercial supplier of a component (e.g., sand used in manufacturing cement, a switch used in an electrical device) is subject to liability if the component itself is defective but not when the component is incorporated into a product that is defective for another reason. However, the commercial supplier of a component may be liable if that supplier substantially participates in the process of integrating the component into the design of the assembled product and that product is defective because of the integration.

However, the commercial supplier of a component may be liable if that supplier substantially participates in the process of integrating the component into the design of the assembled product and that product is defective because of the integration.

Lessor
Generally, a lessor of a commercial product (e.g., car, boat, tools) is subject to strict liability for a defective product.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A seller that provides both products and services generally is liable if the defective product is consumed (e.g., food at a restaurant) but not if the product is only used (e.g., the vendor of a balloon ride when the balloon itself is defective). A product is tangible personal property distributed commercially for use or consumption. A service is not a product. Hospitals and doctors generally are treated as providing a service, rather than a product, in cases in which the defective product is used as a tool, loaned to the patient, or even implanted in the patient.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A seller that provides both products and services generally is liable if the defective product is consumed (e.g., food at a restaurant) but not if the product is only used...
rules.source_text:
Products And Services
Rule A seller that provides both products and services generally is liable if the defective product is consumed (e.g., food at a restaurant) but not if the product is only used (e.g., the vendor of a balloon ride when the balloon itself is defective). A product is tangible personal property distributed commercially for use or consumption. A service is not a product. Hospitals and doctors generally are treated as providing a service, rather than a product, in cases in which the defective product is used as a tool, loaned to the patient, or even implanted in the patient.

rules.rule_statement: Because the seller must be in the business of selling similar products, a casual seller, such as an individual car owner who sells a car to his neighbor or an accountant who sells her office furniture to another businessperson, is not subject to strict liability. Similarly, an auctioneer of a product generally is not subject to strict liability for the products auctioned. The immunity created by workers’ compensation statutes protects the plaintiff’s employer from most tort claims brought by the victim. It does not provide any immunity for other defendants. Frequently, the plaintiff-employee is injured while working with a defective machine tool or with a toxic substance, such as asbestos insulation. Workers’ compensation does not bar a claim against the manufacturer of these products.
rules.short_rule_statement: Because the seller must be in the business of selling similar products, a casual seller, such as an individual car owner who sells a car to his neighbor or an accountant who...
rules.source_text:
Exclusions
Because the seller must be in the business of selling similar products, a casual seller, such as an individual car owner who sells a car to his neighbor or an accountant who sells her office furniture to another businessperson, is not subject to strict liability. Similarly, an auctioneer of a product generally is not subject to strict liability for the products auctioned. The immunity created by workers’ compensation statutes protects the plaintiff’s employer from most tort claims brought by the victim. It does not provide any immunity for other defendants. Frequently, the plaintiff-employee is injured while working with a defective machine tool or with a toxic substance, such as asbestos insulation. Workers’ compensation does not bar a claim against the manufacturer of these products.

rules.rule_statement: As with negligence claims, the plaintiff is entitled to recover damages for any personal injury or property damage. A claim for purely economic loss generally is not allowed under a strict-liability theory but must be brought as a breach?of?warranty action, as must a claim for harm to the product itself and any consequential damages arising therefrom.
rules.short_rule_statement: As with negligence claims, the plaintiff is entitled to recover damages for any personal injury or property damage. A claim for purely economic loss generally is not allowed...
rules.source_text:
Damages
As with negligence claims, the plaintiff is entitled to recover damages for any personal injury or property damage. A claim for purely economic loss generally is not allowed under a strict-liability theory but must be brought as a breach?of?warranty action, as must a claim for harm to the product itself and any consequential damages arising therefrom.

rules.rule_statement: When a plaintiff can show that a defective product produced by several manufacturers was fungible in its relation to its capacity to cause harm, the court may permit the jury to apportion damages on the basis of each manufacturer’s share of the market for the product.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a plaintiff can show that a defective product produced by several manufacturers was fungible in its relation to its capacity to cause harm, the court may permit the jury...
rules.source_text:
Market Share Liability
When a plaintiff can show that a defective product produced by several manufacturers was fungible in its relation to its capacity to cause harm, the court may permit the jury to apportion damages on the basis of each manufacturer’s share of the market for the product.

rules.rule_statement: In most states and under the Third Restatement, the plaintiff’s own negligence reduces his recovery in an action for strict products liability in the same manner as in a negligence action. In those jurisdictions, a plaintiff’s conduct that amounts to an assumption of the risk is treated as comparative negligence to reduce but not eliminate recovery. In some comparative-fault jurisdictions, the plaintiff’s recovery in strict products liability is not reduced by the percentage that his fault contributed to causing his injury.
rules.short_rule_statement: In most states and under the Third Restatement, the plaintiff’s own negligence reduces his recovery in an action for strict products liability in the same manner as in a...
rules.source_text:
Comparative Fault
In most states and under the Third Restatement, the plaintiff’s own negligence reduces his recovery in an action for strict products liability in the same manner as in a negligence action. In those jurisdictions, a plaintiff’s conduct that amounts to an assumption of the risk is treated as comparative negligence to reduce but not eliminate recovery. In some comparative-fault jurisdictions, the plaintiff’s recovery in strict products liability is not reduced by the percentage that his fault contributed to causing his injury.

rules.rule_statement: In a contributory-negligence jurisdiction, the plaintiff’s negligence generally is not a defense to an action for strict products liability when the plaintiff negligently failed to discover the defect or misused the product in a reasonably foreseeable way, but it generally is when the plaintiff’s fault consisted of unreasonably proceeding despite a known product defect. Suppliers are required to anticipate reasonably foreseeable misuses of their products.
rules.short_rule_statement: In a contributory-negligence jurisdiction, the plaintiff’s negligence generally is not a defense to an action for strict products liability when the plaintiff negligently...
rules.source_text:
Contributory Negligence
In a contributory-negligence jurisdiction, the plaintiff’s negligence generally is not a defense to an action for strict products liability when the plaintiff negligently failed to discover the defect or misused the product in a reasonably foreseeable way, but it generally is when the plaintiff’s fault consisted of unreasonably proceeding despite a known product defect. Suppliers are required to anticipate reasonably foreseeable misuses of their products.

rules.rule_statement: Assumption of the risk is a subjective standard. The plaintiff must be aware of the danger and knowingly expose himself to it. Voluntary and knowing assumption of the risk is a complete bar to recovery in contributory-negligence jurisdictions and in a small number of comparative-fault jurisdictions. Because traditional assumption of the risk has been eliminated in most comparative- fault jurisdictions, in those states, a plaintiff’s assumption of a risk reduces his recovery in proportion to his degree of fault, but it is not a complete bar to recovery.
rules.short_rule_statement: Assumption of the risk is a subjective standard. The plaintiff must be aware of the danger and knowingly expose himself to it. Voluntary and knowing assumption of the risk is a...
rules.source_text:
Assumption Of The Risk
Assumption of the risk is a subjective standard. The plaintiff must be aware of the danger and knowingly expose himself to it. Voluntary and knowing assumption of the risk is a complete bar to recovery in contributory-negligence jurisdictions and in a small number of comparative-fault jurisdictions. Because traditional assumption of the risk has been eliminated in most comparative- fault jurisdictions, in those states, a plaintiff’s assumption of a risk reduces his recovery in proportion to his degree of fault, but it is not a complete bar to recovery.

rules.canonical_name: Product Misuse, Modiﬁcation, Or Alteration By The User
rules.rule_statement: The misuse, alteration, or modification of a product by the user in a manner that is neither intended by nor reasonably foreseeable to the manufacturer typically negates liability. However, foreseeable misuse, alteration, or modification usually does not preclude recovery. A majority of comparative-fault jurisdictions treat product misuse as a form of fault that reduces but does not eliminate the plaintiff’s recovery. A significant minority of comparative-fault jurisdictions and most contributory-negligence jurisdictions hold that product misuse totally bars recovery.
rules.short_rule_statement: The misuse, alteration, or modification of a product by the user in a manner that is neither intended by nor reasonably foreseeable to the manufacturer typically negates...
rules.source_text:
Product Misuse, Modiﬁcation, Or Alteration By The User
The misuse, alteration, or modification of a product by the user in a manner that is neither intended by nor reasonably foreseeable to the manufacturer typically negates liability. However, foreseeable misuse, alteration, or modification usually does not preclude recovery. A majority of comparative-fault jurisdictions treat product misuse as a form of fault that reduces but does not eliminate the plaintiff’s recovery. A significant minority of comparative-fault jurisdictions and most contributory-negligence jurisdictions hold that product misuse totally bars recovery.

rules.rule_statement: If the product substantially changes between the time it is distributed by the manufacturer and the time it reaches the consumer (e.g., a part is reconditioned), then this change may constitute a superseding cause that cuts off the liability of the original manufacturer.
rules.short_rule_statement: If the product substantially changes between the time it is distributed by the manufacturer and the time it reaches the consumer (e.g., a part is reconditioned), then this...
rules.source_text:
Substantial Change In The Product
If the product substantially changes between the time it is distributed by the manufacturer and the time it reaches the consumer (e.g., a part is reconditioned), then this change may constitute a superseding cause that cuts off the liability of the original manufacturer.

rules.rule_statement: Most often, compliance with governmental safety standards is not conclusive evidence that the product is not defective. On the other hand, to determine whether the product is defective, the jury can consider evidence introduced by the defendant that the product complied with governmental standards as well as evidence offered by the plaintiff on the product’s failure to comply with these standards. However, if a product complies with federal safety statutes or regulations, a state tort claim act may be preempted if (1) Congress has explicitly so indicated, (2) Congress has comprehensively regulated the field (i.e., field preemption), or (3) it would be impossible for the manufacturer to comply with both the federal regulation and the requirements of state tort law.
rules.short_rule_statement: Most often, compliance with governmental safety standards is not conclusive evidence that the product is not defective. On the other hand, to determine whether the product is...
rules.source_text:
Compliance With Governmental Standards
Most often, compliance with governmental safety standards is not conclusive evidence that the product is not defective. On the other hand, to determine whether the product is defective, the jury can consider evidence introduced by the defendant that the product complied with governmental standards as well as evidence offered by the plaintiff on the product’s failure to comply with these standards. However, if a product complies with federal safety statutes or regulations, a state tort claim act may be preempted if (1) Congress has explicitly so indicated, (2) Congress has comprehensively regulated the field (i.e., field preemption), or (3) it would be impossible for the manufacturer to comply with both the federal regulation and the requirements of state tort law.

rules.rule_statement: In failure-to-warn and design-defect cases, the manufacturer may introduce as evidence the level of relevant scientific, technological, and safety knowledge existing and reasonably feasible at the time of the product’s distribution. In most jurisdictions, compliance with this state-of-the-art standard does not bar recovery against the manufacturer as a matter of law. However, many states have enacted statutes providing that compliance with the state-of-the-art standard is a total bar to recovery.
rules.short_rule_statement: In failure-to-warn and design-defect cases, the manufacturer may introduce as evidence the level of relevant scientific, technological, and safety knowledge existing and...
rules.source_text:
State-Of-The-Art Standard
In failure-to-warn and design-defect cases, the manufacturer may introduce as evidence the level of relevant scientific, technological, and safety knowledge existing and reasonably feasible at the time of the product’s distribution. In most jurisdictions, compliance with this state-of-the-art standard does not bar recovery against the manufacturer as a matter of law. However, many states have enacted statutes providing that compliance with the state-of-the-art standard is a total bar to recovery.

rules.rule_statement: The statute of limitations begins to run against the plaintiff with a personal injury whenever he discovers or, in the exercise of reasonable care, should discover his injury and its connection to the product. As a result, the statute of limitations may not preclude an action against a manufacturer or other seller until many decades after the manufacture and distribution of the product. For example, asbestos-related diseases may not manifest until decades after the asbestos insulation is distributed and the plaintiff is exposed to it.

Statute Of Limitations Issues
The statute of limitations begins to run against the plaintiff with a personal injury whenever he discovers or, in the exercise of reasonable care, should discover his injury and its connection to the product. As a result, the statute of limitations may not preclude an action against a manufacturer or other seller until many decades after the manufacture and distribution of the product. For example, asbestos-related diseases may not manifest until decades after the asbestos insulation is distributed and the plaintiff is exposed to it.

rules.rule_statement: A disclaimer or limitation of remedies or other contractual exculpation (i.e., waiver) by a product seller or other distributor does not generally bar or reduce an otherwise valid products-liability claim for personal injury.
rules.short_rule_statement: A disclaimer or limitation of remedies or other contractual exculpation (i.e., waiver) by a product seller or other distributor does not generally bar or reduce an otherwise...
rules.source_text:
Contract Disclaimers, Limitations, And Waivers
A disclaimer or limitation of remedies or other contractual exculpation (i.e., waiver) by a product seller or other distributor does not generally bar or reduce an otherwise valid products-liability claim for personal injury.

rules.rule_statement: As with negligence, the defendant’s liability can be cut off by unforeseeable intervening causes. See § IV.E.3.b.2)b) Unforeseeable Intervening Causes, supra.
rules.short_rule_statement: As with negligence, the defendant’s liability can be cut off by unforeseeable intervening causes. See § IV.E.3.b.2)b) Unforeseeable Intervening Causes, supra.

Unforeseeable Intervening Causes
As with negligence, the defendant’s liability can be cut off by unforeseeable intervening causes. See § IV.E.3.b.2)b) Unforeseeable Intervening Causes, supra.

rules.rule_statement: Products-liability actions brought under warranty theories generally may be brought against not only a retailer of a product but also a manufacturer or distributor of goods, at least when damages are sought for personal injury or property damage.
rules.short_rule_statement: Products-liability actions brought under warranty theories generally may be brought against not only a retailer of a product but also a manufacturer or distributor of goods, at...
rules.source_text:
Warranties
Products-liability actions brought under warranty theories generally may be brought against not only a retailer of a product but also a manufacturer or distributor of goods, at least when damages are sought for personal injury or property damage.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The implied warranty of merchantability warrants that the product being sold is generally acceptable and reasonably fit for the ordinary purposes for which it is being sold. For the implied warranty of merchantability to apply, the seller must be a merchant of the kind of goods at issue. Rule The implied warranty of fitness warrants that a product is fit for a particular purpose. The implied warranty of fitness applies only if the seller knows the particular purpose for which the product is being purchased and the buyer relies on the seller’s skill or judgment in supplying the product.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule The implied warranty of merchantability warrants that the product being sold is generally acceptable and reasonably fit for the ordinary purposes for which it is being...
rules.source_text:
Two Types
Rule The implied warranty of merchantability warrants that the product being sold is generally acceptable and reasonably fit for the ordinary purposes for which it is being sold. For the implied warranty of merchantability to apply, the seller must be a merchant of the kind of goods at issue. Rule The implied warranty of fitness warrants that a product is fit for a particular purpose. The implied warranty of fitness applies only if the seller knows the particular purpose for which the product is being purchased and the buyer relies on the seller’s skill or judgment in supplying the product.

rules.rule_statement: Any product that fails to live up to either of the above warranties constitutes a breach of the defendant’s warranty; the plaintiff need not prove any fault on the defendant’s part. The buyer may recover damages for personal injury and property damage, as well as for purely economic loss.

Damages

rules.rule_statement: The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) includes three alternative provisions that extend privity to a plaintiff who is not the buyer and permit recovery for breach of an implied warranty. The majority of states have adopted Alternative A. Some states have not adopted any alternative. Others have adopted a modified version. Alternatives for Privity Requirement for Breach of Implied Warranty Allows a member of the buyer’s family or household to recover for Alternative A personal injury but not for property damage or pure economic loss (majority trend) (most restrictive) Allows anyone reasonably expected to use, consume, or be affected Alternative B by the product to recover for personal injury only Allows anyone reasonably expected to use, consume, or be affected Alternative C by the product to recover for personal injury, property damage, and economic loss

rules.rule_statement: An express warranty is a guarantee—an affirmation of fact or a promise—made by a seller regarding the product that is part of the basis of a bargain. The seller is liable for any breach of the express warranty, regardless of fault. Damages for personal injury or property damage are recoverable.
rules.short_rule_statement: An express warranty is a guarantee—an affirmation of fact or a promise—made by a seller regarding the product that is part of the basis of a bargain. The seller is liable for...
rules.source_text:
Express Warranties
An express warranty is a guarantee—an affirmation of fact or a promise—made by a seller regarding the product that is part of the basis of a bargain. The seller is liable for any breach of the express warranty, regardless of fault. Damages for personal injury or property damage are recoverable.

rules.rule_statement: Although the seller generally can disclaim warranties, in the case of consumer goods, any limitation of consequential damages for personal injury is prima facie unconscionable. Disclaimer clauses that conflict with the express warranties, such as “All warranties, express or implied, are disclaimed,” are ignored. In the case of express warranties, a disclaimer is valid only if it is consistent with the warranty, which it usually is not.
rules.short_rule_statement: Although the seller generally can disclaim warranties, in the case of consumer goods, any limitation of consequential damages for personal injury is prima facie unconscionable....

Disclaimers
Although the seller generally can disclaim warranties, in the case of consumer goods, any limitation of consequential damages for personal injury is prima facie unconscionable. Disclaimer clauses that conflict with the express warranties, such as “All warranties, express or implied, are disclaimed,” are ignored. In the case of express warranties, a disclaimer is valid only if it is consistent with the warranty, which it usually is not.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff’s unreasonable, voluntary encountering of a known product risk affects recovery in the same way it would in a claim for strict products liability. Most comparative-fault jurisdictions reduce recovery based on warranty claims in the same way they would for a claim of strict products liability. In contributory-negligence jurisdictions, most courts hold that contributory negligence does not bar a plaintiff’s warranty claim, except when the contributory negligence consists of the unreasonable encountering of a known risk (i.e., the overlap between contributory negligence and assumption of the risk). With or without using the language “product misuse,” most courts find that product misuse prevents recovery under the implied warranty of merchantability when the product is warranted to be fit for ordinary purposes. A warranty claim generally fails if the plaintiff fails to provide the seller with notice of the breach of warranty within the statutorily required period (when applicable) or a reasonable period.

With or without using the language “product misuse,” most courts find that product misuse prevents recovery under the implied warranty of merchantability when the product is warranted to be fit for ordinary purposes. rules.rule_statement: Rule A plaintiff may bring an action for defamation if the defendant’s defamatory language: Is of or concerning the plaintiff; Is published to a third party who understands its defamatory nature; and Damages the plaintiff’s reputation. For matters of public concern, the plaintiff is constitutionally required to prove fault on the part of the defendant. If the plaintiff is either a public official or a public figure, then the plaintiff must prove actual malice. If either (1) the defamatory statement relates to a matter of public concern or (2) the plaintiff is a public official or a public figure, then the plaintiff must prove that the defamatory statement is false as part of the prima facie case.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A plaintiff may bring an action for defamation if the defendant’s defamatory language: Is of or concerning the plaintiff; Is published to a third party who understands its...
rules.source_text:
Defamation
Rule A plaintiff may bring an action for defamation if the defendant’s defamatory language: Is of or concerning the plaintiff; Is published to a third party who understands its defamatory nature; and Damages the plaintiff’s reputation. For matters of public concern, the plaintiff is constitutionally required to prove fault on the part of the defendant. If the plaintiff is either a public official or a public figure, then the plaintiff must prove actual malice. If either (1) the defamatory statement relates to a matter of public concern or (2) the plaintiff is a public official or a public figure, then the plaintiff must prove that the defamatory statement is false as part of the prima facie case.

rules.rule_statement: Language is defamatory if it harms a person’s reputation by diminishing respect, esteem, or goodwill toward the plaintiff or by deterring others from associating with the plaintiff. The plaintiff may introduce extrinsic facts to establish defamation by innuendo. An opinion is actionable if the defendant implies that there is a factual basis for that opinion. Example 1: The defendant says, “In my opinion, John Jones is a thief.” The statement may be regarded as defamatory because it implies the fact that John Jones stole something. Example 2: The defendant says, “In my opinion, John Jones is a lousy artist.” This statement cannot be the basis for a defamation action because people can disagree regarding the quality of an artist’s paintings.
rules.short_rule_statement: Language is defamatory if it harms a person’s reputation by diminishing respect, esteem, or goodwill toward the plaintiff or by deterring others from associating with the...
rules.source_text:
Defamatory Language
Language is defamatory if it harms a person’s reputation by diminishing respect, esteem, or goodwill toward the plaintiff or by deterring others from associating with the plaintiff. The plaintiff may introduce extrinsic facts to establish defamation by innuendo. An opinion is actionable if the defendant implies that there is a factual basis for that opinion. Example 1: The defendant says, “In my opinion, John Jones is a thief.” The statement may be regarded as defamatory because it implies the fact that John Jones stole something. Example 2: The defendant says, “In my opinion, John Jones is a lousy artist.” This statement cannot be the basis for a defamation action because people can disagree regarding the quality of an artist’s paintings.

rules.rule_statement: A reasonable person must believe that the defamatory communication refers to this particular plaintiff and holds the plaintiff up to scorn or ridicule in the eyes of a substantial number of respectable members of the community. If the defamatory language applies to a group, then a member of the group can maintain a defamation action only if the group is so small that the matter can reasonably be understood to refer to that member, unless there is other evidence that the language refers to that particular member. A deceased individual cannot be defamed. A corporation, partnership, or unincorporated association may be defamed if the language prejudices it in conducting its activities or deters others from dealing with it.
rules.short_rule_statement: A reasonable person must believe that the defamatory communication refers to this particular plaintiff and holds the plaintiff up to scorn or ridicule in the eyes of a...
rules.source_text:
Of Or Concerning The Plaintiff
A reasonable person must believe that the defamatory communication refers to this particular plaintiff and holds the plaintiff up to scorn or ridicule in the eyes of a substantial number of respectable members of the community. If the defamatory language applies to a group, then a member of the group can maintain a defamation action only if the group is so small that the matter can reasonably be understood to refer to that member, unless there is other evidence that the language refers to that particular member. A deceased individual cannot be defamed. A corporation, partnership, or unincorporated association may be defamed if the language prejudices it in conducting its activities or deters others from dealing with it.

rules.rule_statement: Publication of a defamatory matter is the intentional or negligent communication of such matter to a third party (i.e., to someone other than the person being defamed) who understands its defamatory nature. Example: During a private conversation that no one else overhears, an employer falsely states that an employee stole company goods and fires the employee. There has been no publication of the defamatory statement, and the employee does not have a defamation claim. EXAM NOTE: Bar exam questions on defamation often center on the publication requirement.  Remember that the statement must be intentionally or negligently made to a third party.  Beware of fact patterns in which the publication requirement is not met, such as those involving a third party learning about the statement through no fault of the defendant’s, a third party who does not understand the statement to be defamatory, or when no third party hears the statement.
rules.short_rule_statement: Publication of a defamatory matter is the intentional or negligent communication of such matter to a third party (i.e., to someone other than the person being defamed) who...
rules.source_text:
To A Third Party
Publication of a defamatory matter is the intentional or negligent communication of such matter to a third party (i.e., to someone other than the person being defamed) who understands its defamatory nature. Example: During a private conversation that no one else overhears, an employer falsely states that an employee stole company goods and fires the employee. There has been no publication of the defamatory statement, and the employee does not have a defamation claim. EXAM NOTE: Bar exam questions on defamation often center on the publication requirement.  Remember that the statement must be intentionally or negligently made to a third party.  Beware of fact patterns in which the publication requirement is not met, such as those involving a third party learning about the statement through no fault of the defendant’s, a third party who does not understand the statement to be defamatory, or when no third party hears the statement.

rules.rule_statement: A person who repeats a defamatory statement may be liable for defamation even though that person identifies the originator of the statement and expresses a lack of knowledge as to the truthfulness of the statement. In addition, a person who repeats a defamatory statement may be liable for defamation even though the communication of the statement to that person was privileged.

A person who repeats a defamatory statement may be liable for defamation even though that person identifies the originator of the statement and expresses a lack of knowledge as to the truthfulness of the statement. In addition, a person who repeats a defamatory statement may be liable for defamation even though the communication of the statement to that person was privileged.

rules.rule_statement: A federal statute provides that internet service providers are not publishers for the purpose of defamation law.

rules.rule_statement: Since the Supreme Court’s opinion in New York Times v. Sullivan, which held that the First Amendment affects the plaintiff’s right to recover under the common-law tort of defamation, constitutional requirements now underlie many aspects of defamation law. These constitutional requirements affect fundamental aspects of defamation law in various ways depending on (1) the category into which the plaintiff fits and (2) the nature of the defamatory communication.

These constitutional requirements affect fundamental aspects of defamation law in various ways depending on (1) the category into which the plaintiff fits and (2) the nature of the defamatory communication.

rules.rule_statement: The constitutional requirements are the same when the plaintiff is a public figure as when the plaintiff is a public official. There are two types of public figures: influence in society that they are deemed public figures for all purposes. ii) Limited-purpose or special-purpose public figures are defined as plaintiffs who thrust themselves to the forefront of particular public controversies to influence the resolution of the issues involved. Limited-purpose or special-purpose public figures are treated as public figures if the defamatory statement relates to their participation in the controversy, but they are treated as private figures if the defamation relates to any other matter.

Public Figure
The constitutional requirements are the same when the plaintiff is a public figure as when the plaintiff is a public official. There are two types of public figures: influence in society that they are deemed public figures for all purposes. ii) Limited-purpose or special-purpose public figures are defined as plaintiffs who thrust themselves to the forefront of particular public controversies to influence the resolution of the issues involved. Limited-purpose or special-purpose public figures are treated as public figures if the defamatory statement relates to their participation in the controversy, but they are treated as private figures if the defamation relates to any other matter.

rules.rule_statement: If the plaintiff is a private person (neither a public figure nor a public official) and the statement involves a matter of public concern, then the defendant is entitled to limited constitutional protections, though not as significant as those available when the person being defamed is either a public official or public figure. If the plaintiff is a private person and the statement is not a matter of public concern, then there are no constitutional restrictions on the law of defamation. However, many states now apply the same principles of defamation law to all cases involving private persons as plaintiffs.

If the plaintiff is a private person and the statement is not a matter of public concern, then there are no constitutional restrictions on the law of defamation. However, many states now apply the same principles of defamation law to all cases involving private persons as plaintiffs.

rules.rule_statement: Rule If the plaintiff in a defamation action is either a public official or a public figure, then the plaintiff is required to prove that the defendant acted with actual malice; that is, he either had knowledge that the statement was false or acted with reckless disregard as to the truthfulness of the statement. To establish actual malice based on a reckless disregard for the truthfulness of a statement, the plaintiff must prove that the defendant entertained serious doubts about its truthfulness; mere failure to check facts is not sufficient.

Public Ofﬁcial Or Public Figure
Rule If the plaintiff in a defamation action is either a public official or a public figure, then the plaintiff is required to prove that the defendant acted with actual malice; that is, he either had knowledge that the statement was false or acted with reckless disregard as to the truthfulness of the statement. To establish actual malice based on a reckless disregard for the truthfulness of a statement, the plaintiff must prove that the defendant entertained serious doubts about its truthfulness; mere failure to check facts is not sufficient.

The level of fault will determine what damages may be recovered.

rules.canonical_name: Private Person/Not A Matter Of Public Concern
rules.rule_statement: If the plaintiff in a defamation action is a private person and the defendant’s statement does not involve a matter of public concern, then the constitutional requirements do not apply. At common law, the defendant was strictly liable. Most states today require at least negligence by the defendant for all defamation actions, and some now require actual malice in all defamation actions. Consequently, a defendant who reasonably believed that the statement was true is not liable for defamation.
rules.short_rule_statement: If the plaintiff in a defamation action is a private person and the defendant’s statement does not involve a matter of public concern, then the constitutional requirements do...
rules.source_text:
Private Person/Not A Matter Of Public Concern
If the plaintiff in a defamation action is a private person and the defendant’s statement does not involve a matter of public concern, then the constitutional requirements do not apply. At common law, the defendant was strictly liable. Most states today require at least negligence by the defendant for all defamation actions, and some now require actual malice in all defamation actions. Consequently, a defendant who reasonably believed that the statement was true is not liable for defamation.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Defamation by words written, printed, or otherwise recorded in permanent form is libel. Today, it is generally—though not universally—accepted that defamatory radio and television broadcasts are libel, regardless of whether they are spoken from a script. Most courts addressing the issue have held that email messages are categorized as libel. It is not yet clear whether courts will hold that social media posts and text messages are libel or slander. Subject to the constitutionally imposed limits on damages recoverable in a defamation action, the libel plaintiff may recover general damages, which are any damages that compensate the plaintiff for harm to the plaintiff’s reputation. Under common law, the plaintiff was entitled to recover presumed damages as part of general damages. The plaintiff did not need to prove actual damages; the plaintiff’s lawyer only needed to invite the jury to award the damages that it believed flowed from the defendant’s defamatory communication. See § 8. Constitutional Limitations on Damages, below. Rule In some jurisdictions, under the doctrine of libel per quod, if the nature of the defamatory statement requires proof of extrinsic facts to show that the statement is defamatory, then the plaintiff must prove either special damages or that the statement fits into one of the four categories of statements that satisfy the requirements of slander per se.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Defamation by spoken word, gesture, or any form other than libel is slander. To recover for slander, the plaintiff must prove special damages or slander per se. Rule Special damages require the plaintiff to prove that a third party heard the defendant’s defamatory comments and acted adversely to the plaintiff. Most often, special damages involve an economic loss to the plaintiff (e.g., loss of employment or loss of business), but they also would include such things as the plaintiff’s fiancé breaking off the engagement or a friend refusing to host the plaintiff in her home after hearing the defamatory comments. Rule Under the doctrine of slander per se, a plaintiff alleging slander need not prove special damages if the statement defaming the plaintiff fits into one of four categories. These categories comprise accusing the plaintiff of (1) committing a crime, (2) conduct reflecting on the plaintiff’s lack of fitness to conduct his business, trade, or profession, (3) having a loathsome disease, or (4) sexual misconduct. To qualify as slander per se, the defamatory statement must accuse the plaintiff of one of the following: imprisonment; ii) Conduct reflecting on the plaintiff’s lack of fitness to conduct his business, trade, or profession: A general disparaging statement (e.g., “The plaintiff is a drunk”) must be particularly harmful to one engaged in the plaintiff’s business, trade, or profession; iii) Having a loathsome disease: Traditionally, loathsome diseases included illnesses such as leprosy or a sexually transmitted disease; and iv) Sexual misconduct: In modern times, examples of cases falling within this subcategory, as well as the previous one, are very rare. A few courts have held that trading sex for drugs constitutes sexual misconduct. At common law, once a plaintiff satisfied the requirements of slander by proving either special damages or slander per se, then general damages as parasitic damages could be recovered. Damages recoverable in a slander action, as well as damages recoverable in a libel action, are subject to the constitutional limitations discussed below.

Constitutional Limitations On Damages
If the plaintiff is a private figure and the matter is one of public concern, the plaintiff must prove actual malice by the defendant to recover presumed or punitive damages. However, if the plaintiff is a private figure and the matter is not one of public concern, then the plaintiff need not prove actual malice to recover presumed or punitive damages.

rules.rule_statement: A truthful statement is not defamatory. A statement that is substantially true, as judged by the harm to the plaintiff’s reputation, is generally not defamatory, even though some details are inaccurate. A statement that a person has engaged in conduct that substantially differs from their actual conduct is not considered true, even if the person’s actual conduct was equally or more morally reprehensible. EXAM NOTE: If a statement is true but seems like defamation, consider whether it constitutes intentional inﬂiction of emotional distress or invasion of privacy.

rules.canonical_name: In The Interest Of The Publisher (Defendant), Such As Defending The...
rules.rule_statement: ii) In the interest of the recipient of the statement or a third party; or iii) Affecting an important public interest. Qualified privileges most often occur in the context of employment references, credit reports, and charges and accusations within professional societies and among members of religious and charitable organizations. Statements must be made in good faith to be protected by qualified privilege. A qualified privilege may be lost if it is abused. Generally, a privilege is abused by making statements outside the scope of the privilege or by acting with malice. Traditionally, the malice required was express malice—hatred, ill will, or spite. Today, most jurisdictions hold that actual malice (i.e., knowing that a statement is false or acting with a reckless disregard as to the truthfulness of the statement), will defeat a qualified privilege. The burden is on the defendant to prove that a privilege, whether absolute or qualified, exists. It is, therefore, an affirmative defense. The burden is then on the plaintiff to prove that the privilege has been abused and therefore lost. See Milkovich v. Lorian Journal Co., 497 U.S. 1 (1990); Gertz v. Welch, Inc., 418 U.S. 323 (1974). Gertz v. Robert Welch, Inc., 418 U.S. 323 (1974). Dun & Bradstreet, Inc. v. Greenmoss Builders, Inc., 472 U.S. 749 (1985).

rules.rule_statement: Invasion of privacy is not a single tort but includes four separate causes of action: Misappropriation of the right to publicity Intrusion upon seclusion Placing the plaintiff in false light Public disclosure of private facts The right of privacy does not extend to corporations, only to individuals. Additionally, because the right of privacy is a personal right, in most instances, this right terminates upon the plaintiff’s death and does not extend to family members. Mnemonic for privacy actions: I FLAP – Intrusion, False Light, Appropriation, Private facts

rules.rule_statement: Many states recognize an action for unreasonable intrusion upon the plaintiff’s private affairs (also referred to as “intrusion upon seclusion”). Rule To recover for intrusion upon seclusion, a plaintiff must prove that: seclusion; and ii) The intrusion would be highly offensive to a reasonable person. Eavesdropping on private conversations by electronic devices is considered an unreasonable intrusion. Photographing a person in a public place generally is not, unless the photograph is taken in a manner that reveals information about the person that the person expects to keep private even in a public place. Unlike the other forms of invasion of privacy, no publication is required to establish liability.

rules.canonical_name: Made Public Facts About The Plaintiff That Placed The Plaintiff In A False...
rules.rule_statement: ii) Such false light would be highly offensive to a reasonable person. Most jurisdictions also require that the plaintiff prove actual malice by the defendant. As considered in the discussion of defamation, proof of actual malice may be constitutionally required in many instances. Attributing to the plaintiff views that the plaintiff does not hold or actions that the plaintiff did not take may constitute placing the plaintiff in a false light. Similarly, falsely asserting that the plaintiff was a victim of a crime or once lived in poverty may be sufficient for the false light tort. This false light does not necessarily involve an absolute falsity; rather, the presentation of the truth in a misleading way can be sufficient to sustain this claim. Consequently, truth is not a defense if the resulting implication is still false or misleading, but it may be a defense if the resulting implication is true.

The information must be communicated at large or to so many people that it is substantially certain to become public knowledge.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A prima facie case of intentional misrepresentation (also called “fraud” or “deceit”) is established by proof that: The defendant knowingly or recklessly misrepresented a material fact, opinion, intention, or law with the intent to induce the plaintiff’s reliance; and The plaintiff justifiably relied on the defendant’s misrepresentation and suffered damages as a result.

Intentional Misrepresentation

rules.rule_statement: A misrepresentation must be of a material fact, opinion, intention, or law. Usually, a defendant actively misrepresents the facts, such as through deceptive or misleading statements or pictures. Sometimes, the misrepresentation occurs through conduct, such as the active concealment of a material fact (e.g., the seller of a house places paneling over the basement walls to conceal the condition of the foundation).

rules.rule_statement: A misrepresentation is material if a reasonable person would give it significant weight in deciding whether to enter into the transaction or if the defendant knew that the plaintiff would give it such weight. Puffery is broad, exaggerated statements as to the quality of an item (e.g., seller of a bond states, “It’s the safest investment”). Puffery is not material.

Puffery is broad, exaggerated statements as to the quality of an item (e.g., seller of a bond states, “It’s the safest investment”). Puffery is not material.

Causation

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff’s reliance must have been justifiable. Reliance is not justifiable if the facts are obviously false or if the defendant is stating a lay opinion. However, the plaintiff is under no duty to investigate the truthfulness of the statement.

Justiﬁable Reliance
The plaintiff’s reliance must have been justifiable. Reliance is not justifiable if the facts are obviously false or if the defendant is stating a lay opinion. However, the plaintiff is under no duty to investigate the truthfulness of the statement.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff must prove actual damages to recover; nominal damages are not awarded. Consequential damages may also be awarded. Most courts limit damages to the economic loss suffered by the plaintiff but do not require the plaintiff to have realized that loss (e.g., sold the misrepresented item at a loss or incurred the expense to repair the item).

Damages
The plaintiff must prove actual damages to recover; nominal damages are not awarded. Consequential damages may also be awarded. Most courts limit damages to the economic loss suffered by the plaintiff but do not require the plaintiff to have realized that loss (e.g., sold the misrepresented item at a loss or incurred the expense to repair the item).

rules.rule_statement: In most jurisdictions, recovery in misrepresentation cases is based on the benefit-of-the-bargain measure (i.e., the difference between the actual value received in the transaction and the value that would have been received if the misrepresentation were true). In some jurisdictions, instead of basing the loss-of-bargain measure on the value of what was received, an amount equal to the cost of conforming the property to what was promised can be used. In some other jurisdictions, recovery is based on the out-of-pocket measure (i.e., the difference between the amount paid and the market value of what was received). In a few jurisdictions, the defrauded person can elect which measure to use.

Measure Of Damages

In some jurisdictions, instead of basing the loss-of-bargain measure on the value of what was received, an amount equal to the cost of conforming the property to what was promised can be used. In some other jurisdictions, recovery is based on the out-of-pocket measure (i.e., the difference between the amount paid and the market value of what was received). In a few jurisdictions, the defrauded person can elect which measure to use.

rules.rule_statement: The tort of negligent misrepresentation is based on a breach of the duty to supply correct information. Rule The elements of negligent misrepresentation are: The defendant, in the course of business, profession, or employment or in a transaction in which the defendant has a pecuniary interest: Provided false information to guide the plaintiff; Because of the defendant’s failure to use reasonable care in obtaining or communicating the information; and The plaintiff reasonably relied upon the information; and This reliance caused the plaintiff to suffer pecuniary loss.
rules.short_rule_statement: The tort of negligent misrepresentation is based on a breach of the duty to supply correct information. Rule The elements of negligent misrepresentation are: The defendant, in...
rules.source_text:
Negligent Misrepresentation
The tort of negligent misrepresentation is based on a breach of the duty to supply correct information. Rule The elements of negligent misrepresentation are: The defendant, in the course of business, profession, or employment or in a transaction in which the defendant has a pecuniary interest: Provided false information to guide the plaintiff; Because of the defendant’s failure to use reasonable care in obtaining or communicating the information; and The plaintiff reasonably relied upon the information; and This reliance caused the plaintiff to suffer pecuniary loss.

rules.rule_statement: To recover, a plaintiff must be the person or one of a limited group of persons for whose guidance the information was intended. In addition, reliance on the information must be in a transaction that the defendant intended to influence, or that the defendant knew the recipient intended to influence, or in a substantially similar transaction. Example: An accountant furnishes a financial statement to a corporation that, because of her negligence, overstates the corporation’s assets. The corporation does not inform the accountant that the corporation will use the statement to obtain a loan from a bank. The bank relies on the erroneous statement in making a loan to the corporation that is secured by the corporation’s assets. The corporation is unable to repay the loan, and the bank cannot fully recover the unpaid balance of the loan by exercising its security interest in the corporation’s assets. The bank sues the accountant for negligent misrepresentation. The accountant is not liable to the bank for the bank’s loss. A negligent misrepresentation made during negotiation or performance of a contract does not give rise to tort liability; recovery for economic losses is governed by contract law.

rules.rule_statement: When physical harm rather than pecuniary loss is a foreseeable result of a negligent misrepresentation, the ordinary rules of negligence generally apply, but damages for emotional distress are not permitted. Example: A defendant air traffic controller is liable for ordinary negligence when he negligently gives the pilot of an airplane incorrect information about the plane’s location and speed, and as a result, the passenger-parachutist jumps to his death in a lake instead of at the target airfield.

rules.rule_statement: Rule In the majority of states, the contract in question must be valid and not terminable at will. However, a minority of states will allow the cause of action to be brought for interference with a contract that is terminable at will. A contract that is voidable by one of the parties to the contract, such as because of a violation of the Statute of Frauds, may be the subject of tortious interference unless the party elects to void the contract.

To be considered tortious, a defendant’s actions must substantially exceed fair competition and free expression, such as persuading a bank not to lend money to a competitor.

rules.rule_statement: A defendant’s interference usually will be found to be justified if it is not motivated by an improper purpose. Some courts require the plaintiff to prove that the breach was induced by an improper purpose. Considerations of health, safety, morals, or ending poor labor conditions are proper purposes. Example: A defendant who tries to convince a U.S. clothing store to stop buying fabrics from a foreign textile manufacturer known for its inhumane labor conditions will not be liable for interference with a contract. A defendant might claim that the interference is within the privilege of fair competition. Rule Under the privilege of fair competition, a defendant who intentionally causes a third person to either (1) not enter into a contractual relationship or (2) terminate an existing at-will contract is not liable for improper conduct if:

rules.canonical_name: Interference With A Prospective Economic Advantage
rules.rule_statement: Rule A defendant may be liable for intentionally interfering with a prospective business relationship or economic benefit between the plaintiff and a third party, even in the absence of an existing contract. The other elements remain the same, but without an existing contract, some jurisdictions require that the defendant’s conduct be wrongful, either independently tortious (e.g., consisting of fraud or assault) or a violation of federal or state law. Other jurisdictions and the Second Restatement engage in a more open-ended balancing process to decide whether the defendant’s conduct is improper. Absent proof of wrongful conduct (e.g., defamation), a defendant who is the plaintiff’s business competitor will not be held liable for encouraging the third party to switch his business to the defendant.

rules.source_text:
Interference With A Prospective Economic Advantage
Rule A defendant may be liable for intentionally interfering with a prospective business relationship or economic benefit between the plaintiff and a third party, even in the absence of an existing contract. The other elements remain the same, but without an existing contract, some jurisdictions require that the defendant’s conduct be wrongful, either independently tortious (e.g., consisting of fraud or assault) or a violation of federal or state law. Other jurisdictions and the Second Restatement engage in a more open-ended balancing process to decide whether the defendant’s conduct is improper. Absent proof of wrongful conduct (e.g., defamation), a defendant who is the plaintiff’s business competitor will not be held liable for encouraging the third party to switch his business to the defendant.

rules.canonical_name: Wrongful Use Of The Legal System
rules.rule_statement: A defendant’s wrongful or misplaced use of the legal system can result in tort liability. The most common tort actions for such improper conduct are: Malicious prosecution Abuse of process

Wrongful Use Of The Legal System
A defendant’s wrongful or misplaced use of the legal system can result in tort liability. The most common tort actions for such improper conduct are: Malicious prosecution Abuse of process

rules.canonical_name: He Intentionally And Maliciously Institutes, Pursues, Or Causes To Be...
rules.rule_statement: ii) For an improper purpose; iii) A legal action that is brought without probable cause; and iv) That action is dismissed in favor of the person against whom it was brought. Most jurisdictions have extended malicious prosecution to include civil cases as well as criminal actions. The civil action is sometimes known as wrongful institution of civil proceedings. The plaintiff may recover for any damage proximately caused by the malicious prosecution, including legal expenses, lost work time, loss of reputation, and emotional distress. Note that judges and prosecutors enjoy absolute immunity from liability for malicious prosecution.

rules.canonical_name: A Legal Procedure Set In Motion In Proper Form
rules.rule_statement: ii) That is perverted to accomplish an ulterior motive; iii) A willful act perpetrated in the use of process that is not proper in the regular conduct of the proceeding; iv) Causing the plaintiff to sustain damages. For abuse of process, unlike malicious prosecution, the existence of probable cause—and even whether the defendant ultimately prevails on the merits—is not determinative in precluding liability. Rather, the essence of the tort is using the legal process for an ulterior motive, such as extorting payment or recovering property. Example: A local school board of education sued a teacher’s union and subpoenaed 87 teachers for a hearing to prevent the teachers from walking a picket line during a labor dispute between the union and the board of education. Note that abuse of process, like malicious prosecution, does not require ill will or spite, but it does require proof of damages.

A Legal Procedure Set In Motion In Proper Form
ii) That is perverted to accomplish an ulterior motive; iii) A willful act perpetrated in the use of process that is not proper in the regular conduct of the proceeding; iv) Causing the plaintiff to sustain damages. For abuse of process, unlike malicious prosecution, the existence of probable cause—and even whether the defendant ultimately prevails on the merits—is not determinative in precluding liability. Rather, the essence of the tort is using the legal process for an ulterior motive, such as extorting payment or recovering property. Example: A local school board of education sued a teacher’s union and subpoenaed 87 teachers for a hearing to prevent the teachers from walking a picket line during a labor dispute between the union and the board of education. Note that abuse of process, like malicious prosecution, does not require ill will or spite, but it does require proof of damages.
